content: With usual notation, in a triangle ABC $\frac{b+c}{11}=\frac{c+a}{12}=\frac{a+b}{13}$, then the value of $\cos B$ is equal to

In a triangle $A B C$, with usual notations, the sides $\mathrm{a}, \mathrm{b}, \mathrm{c}$ are such that they are roots of the equation $x^3-11 x^2+38 x-40=0$ then $\frac{\cos \mathrm{A}}{\mathrm{a}}+\frac{\cos \mathrm{B}}{\mathrm{b}}+\frac{\cos \mathrm{C}}{\mathrm{c}}=$
explanation:
$$ \begin{aligned} & \text { Given, } x^3-11 x^2+38 x-40=0 \\ & \Rightarrow(x-4)\left(x^2-7 x+10\right)=0 \\ & \Rightarrow(x-4)(x-5)(x-2)=0 \end{aligned} $$

$\mathrm{a}, \mathrm{b}, \mathrm{c}$ are the roots of the given equation.

$$ \Rightarrow a=4, b=5, c=2 $$

By using cosine rule, we get

$$ \begin{aligned} & \frac{\cos A}{a}+\frac{\cos B}{b}+\frac{\cos C}{c} \\ & =\frac{1}{a}\left(\frac{b^2+c^2-a^2}{2 b c}\right)+\frac{1}{b}\left(\frac{c^2+a^2-b^2}{2 c a}\right)+\frac{1}{c}\left(\frac{a^2+b^2-c^2}{2 a b}\right) \\ & =\frac{b^2+c^2-a^2+c^2+a^2-b^2+a^2+b^2-c^2}{2 a b c} \\ & =\frac{a^2+b^2+c^2}{2 a b c} \\ & =\frac{4^2+5^2+2^2}{2 \times 4 \times 5 \times 2} \\ & =\frac{45}{80}=\frac{9}{16} \end{aligned} $$

content: In a triangle $A B C$ with usual notations if $\angle A=30^{\circ}$, then the value of $\left(1+\frac{a}{c}+\frac{b}{c}\right)\left(1+\frac{c}{b}-\frac{a}{b}\right)=$
explanation: $$ \begin{aligned} & \left(1+\frac{a}{c}+\frac{b}{c}\right)\left(1+\frac{c}{b}-\frac{a}{b}\right) \\ & =\left(\frac{c+a+b}{c}\right)\left(\frac{b+c-a}{b}\right) \\ & =\frac{1}{b c}\left[(b+c)^2-a^2\right] \\ & =\frac{1}{b c}\left(b^2+c^2+2 b c-a^2\right) \\ & =\frac{b^2+c^2-a^2}{b c}+2 \\ & =2\left(\frac{b^2+c^2-a^2}{2 b c}\right)+2 \\ & =2 \cos A+2 \,\,\,\,\,\, \,\,\,\,\,\, \,\,\,\,\,\,\text { ...[By cosine rule] } \\ & =2 \cos 30^{\circ}+2 \\ & =2\left(\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right)+2 \\ & =\sqrt{3}+2 \end{aligned} $$

content: In a triangle PQR with usual notations, $\angle \mathrm{R}=\frac{\pi}{2}$. If $\tan \frac{\mathrm{P}}{2}$ and $\tan \frac{\mathrm{Q}}{2}$ are the roots of the equation $a x^2+b x+c=0(a \neq 0)$, then
explanation:
$$ \begin{array}{ll} & \text { In } \triangle \mathrm{PQR}, \\ & \angle \mathrm{P}+\angle \mathrm{Q}+\angle \mathrm{R}=180^{\circ} \\ \therefore & \angle \mathrm{P}+\angle \mathrm{Q}+\frac{\pi}{2}=180^{\circ} \\ \therefore & \angle \mathrm{P}+\angle \mathrm{Q}=\frac{\pi}{2} \\ \therefore & \frac{\angle \mathrm{P}}{2}+\frac{\angle \mathrm{Q}}{2}=\frac{\pi}{4} \end{array} $$

$\tan \left(\frac{\mathrm{P}}{2}\right)$ and $\tan \left(\frac{\mathrm{Q}}{2}\right)$ are the roots of the equation $a x^2+b x+c=0$        ...[Given].

$$ \begin{array}{ll} \therefore \quad & \text { Sum of roots }=\frac{-b}{a} \\ & \Rightarrow \tan \left(\frac{P}{2}\right)+\tan \left(\frac{Q}{2}\right)=\frac{-b}{a} \end{array} $$

Also, $\tan \left(\frac{\mathrm{P}}{2}\right) \cdot \tan \left(\frac{\mathrm{Q}}{2}\right)=\frac{\mathrm{c}}{\mathrm{a}}$

Using, $\tan \left(\frac{P}{2}+\frac{Q}{2}\right)=\frac{\tan \frac{P}{2}+\tan \frac{Q}{2}}{1-\tan \frac{P}{2} \tan \frac{Q}{2}}$, we get

$$ \begin{aligned} & \tan \left(\frac{\pi}{4}\right)=\frac{\frac{-b}{a}}{1-\frac{c}{a}} \\ & \Rightarrow 1=\frac{-b}{a-c} \\ & \Rightarrow a-c=-b \\ & \Rightarrow a+b=c \end{aligned} $$

content: If the angles $\mathrm{A}, \mathrm{B}$ and C of a triangle are in A.P. and if $\mathrm{a}, \mathrm{b}$ and c denote the length of the sides opposite to $\mathrm{A}, \mathrm{B}$ and C respectively, then the value of $\frac{a}{b} \sin 2 B+\frac{b}{a} \sin 2 A$ is
explanation:
$\mathrm{A}, \mathrm{B}, \mathrm{C}$ are in $\mathrm{A} . \mathrm{P}$

$$ \Rightarrow A+C=2 B $$

Also, $\mathrm{A}+\mathrm{B}+\mathrm{C}=180^{\circ}$

$$ \therefore \quad B=60^{\circ} $$

By sine rule,

$$ \begin{aligned} & \frac{\sin A}{a}=\frac{\sin B}{b}=\frac{\sin C}{c}=k \\ & \Rightarrow \sin A=a k, \sin B=b k, \sin C=c k \\ & \frac{a}{-} \sin 2 B+\frac{b}{a} \sin 2 A \end{aligned} $$

$$ \begin{aligned} & =\frac{a}{b}(2 \sin B \cos B)+\frac{b}{a}(2 \sin A \cos A) \\ & =\frac{a}{b}(2 b k \cos B)+\frac{b}{a}(2 a k \cos A) \\ & =2 a k \cos B+2 b k \cos A \\ & =2 k(a \cos B+b \cos A) \\ & =2 k c \quad \therefore[\text { By projection rule }] \\ & =2 \sin C \quad \quad \end{aligned} $$

content: In a triangle $A B C$, with usual notations, if $a=5$, $\mathrm{b}=7 \sin \mathrm{~A}=\frac{3}{4}$, then total number of triangles possible are
explanation:
We know that,

$$ \begin{aligned} & \frac{a}{\sin A}=\frac{b}{\sin B}=\frac{c}{\sin C} \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{5}{\left(\frac{3}{4}\right)}=\frac{7}{\sin B} \\ & \Rightarrow \sin B=\frac{21}{20}>1 \end{aligned} $$

which is not possible since $-1 \leq \sin x \leq 1$

$\therefore \quad$ No triangles can be formed.

content: In a triangle $A B C$, with usual notations, $\cot \left(\frac{A+B}{2}\right) \cdot \tan \left(\frac{A-B}{2}\right)=$
explanation: $$ \begin{aligned} &\text { By Napier's analogy, }\\ &\begin{aligned} & \tan \left(\frac{A-B}{2}\right)=\left(\frac{a-b}{a+b}\right) \cot \left(\frac{C}{2}\right) \\ & \cot \left(\frac{A+B}{2}\right) \tan \left(\frac{A-B}{2}\right) \\ & =\left(\frac{a-b}{a+b}\right) \cot \left(\frac{C}{2}\right) \cot \left(\frac{\pi}{2}-\frac{C}{2}\right) \\ & =\left(\frac{a-b}{a+b}\right) \cot \left(\frac{C}{2}\right) \tan \left(\frac{C}{2}\right)=\frac{a-b}{a+b} \end{aligned} \end{aligned} $$

content: In a triangle ABC , with usual notations, $(\mathrm{a}+\mathrm{b}+\mathrm{c})(\mathrm{a}+\mathrm{b}-\mathrm{c})=3 \mathrm{ab}$, then $\angle \mathrm{C}=$
explanation: $$ \begin{aligned} & (a+b+c)(a+b-c)=3 a b \\ & \Rightarrow(a+b)^2-c^2=3 a b \\ & \Rightarrow a^2+b^2+2 a b-c^2=3 a b \\ & \Rightarrow a^2+b^2-c^2=a b \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{a^2+b^2-c^2}{a b}=1 \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{a^2+b^2-c^2}{2 a b}=\frac{1}{2} \\ & \Rightarrow \cos C=\frac{1}{2} \\ & \Rightarrow C=\frac{\pi}{3} \end{aligned} $$

content: With usual notations in $\triangle \mathrm{ABC}$, if $\angle \mathrm{B}=\frac{\pi}{2}$, and $\tan \frac{\mathrm{A}}{2}, \tan \frac{\mathrm{C}}{2}$ are roots of equation $\mathrm{p} x^2+\mathrm{qx}+\mathrm{r}=0$, $\mathrm{p} \neq 0$, then
explanation:
$$ \begin{array}{ll} & \text { In } \triangle A B C, \\ & \angle A+\angle B+\angle C=180^{\circ} \\ \therefore \quad & \angle A+\frac{\pi}{2}+\angle C=180^{\circ} \\ \therefore \quad & \angle A+\angle C=\frac{\pi}{2} \end{array} $$

$$ \begin{array}{ll} \therefore & \frac{\angle A}{2}+\frac{\angle C}{2}=\frac{\pi}{4} \\ & \tan \left(\frac{A}{2}\right) \text { and } \tan \left(\frac{C}{2}\right) \text { are roots of the equation } \\ & p x^2+q x+r=0 \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\text { ...[Given] }\\ \therefore \quad & \text { Sum of roots }=\frac{-q}{p} \\ & \Rightarrow \tan \left(\frac{A}{2}\right)+\tan \left(\frac{C}{2}\right)=\frac{-q}{p} \\ & \text { Also, } \tan \left(\frac{A}{2}\right) \cdot \tan \left(\frac{C}{2}\right)=\frac{r}{p} \\ & \text { Using, } \tan \left(\frac{A}{2}+\frac{C}{2}\right)=\frac{\tan \frac{A}{2}+\tan \frac{C}{2}}{1-\tan \frac{A}{2} \tan \frac{C}{2}}, \text { we get } \end{array} $$

$$ \begin{aligned} & \tan \left(\frac{\pi}{4}\right)=\frac{\frac{-q}{p}}{1-\frac{r}{p}} \\ & \Rightarrow 1=\frac{-q}{p-r} \\ & \Rightarrow p-r=-q \\ & \Rightarrow p+q=r \end{aligned} $$

content: The circumradius of a triangle whose sides are 10 units, 8 units and 6 units is
explanation:
Let $a=10, b=8, c=6$

By using cosine rule,

$$ \begin{aligned} & \cos A=\frac{b^2+c^2-a^2}{2 b c}=\frac{8^2+6^2-10^2}{2(8)(6)} \\ & \Rightarrow \cos A=0 \\ & \Rightarrow A=90^{\circ} \end{aligned} $$

By sine rule,

$$ \begin{aligned} & 2 R=\frac{a}{\sin A} \\ & \begin{aligned} \Rightarrow R & =\frac{a}{2 \sin A} \\ & =\frac{10}{2 \sin 90^{\circ}} \end{aligned} \end{aligned} $$

$\therefore \quad$ Circumradius $=5$ units

Alternate method:

Let $a=10, b=8, c=6$

Here, $\mathrm{a}^2=\mathrm{b}^2+\mathrm{c}^2$

$\therefore \quad \triangle \mathrm{ABC}$ is a right angled triangle.

In a right angled triangle, the hypotenuse is the diameter of the circumradius.

$$ \text { Circumradius }=\frac{\text { hypotenuse }}{2}=\frac{10}{2}=5 \text { units } $$

content: In a triangle ABC , with usual notations. $\frac{2 \cos \mathrm{~A}}{\mathrm{a}}+\frac{\cos \mathrm{B}}{\mathrm{b}}+\frac{2 \cos \mathrm{C}}{\mathrm{c}}=\frac{\mathrm{a}}{\mathrm{bc}}+\frac{\mathrm{b}}{\mathrm{ca}}$. Then $\angle \mathrm{A}=$
explanation:
$$ \begin{aligned} & \frac{2 \cos A}{a}+\frac{\cos B}{b}+\frac{2 \cos C}{c} \\ & =\frac{2\left(\frac{b^2+c^2-a^2}{2 b c}\right)}{a}+\left(\frac{\frac{a^2+c^2-b^2}{2 a c}}{b}\right)+\frac{2\left(\frac{a^2+b^2-c^2}{2 a b}\right)}{c} \end{aligned} $$

$$ \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\ldots \text { [By cosine rule] } $$

$$ \begin{aligned} & =\frac{2 b^2+2 c^2-2 a^2+a^2+c^2-b^2+2 a^2+2 b^2-2 c^2}{2 a b c} \\ & =\frac{3 b^2+c^2+a^2}{2 a b c} \end{aligned} $$

$$ \begin{aligned} &\begin{aligned} \therefore & \frac{3 b^2+c^2+a^2}{2 a b c}=\frac{a}{b c}+\frac{b}{c a} \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{3 b^2+c^2+a^2}{2 a b c}=\frac{a^2+b^2}{a b c} \\ \Rightarrow & 3 b^2+c^2+a^2=2 a^2+2 b^2 \\ \Rightarrow & a^2=b^2+c^2 \end{aligned}\\ &\Rightarrow \triangle A B C \text { is right angled at } A \text {. }\\ &\Rightarrow \angle A=\frac{\pi}{2} \end{aligned} $$

content: If in triangle ABC , with usual notations $\sin \frac{\mathrm{A}}{2} \cdot \sin \frac{\mathrm{C}}{2}=\sin \frac{\mathrm{B}}{2}$ and 2 s is the perimeter of the triangle, then the value of $s$ is
explanation: $$ \begin{aligned} &\begin{aligned} & \sin \frac{A}{2} \sin \frac{C}{2}=\sin \frac{B}{2} \\ & \Rightarrow \sqrt{\frac{(s-b)(s-c)}{b c}} \sqrt{\frac{(s-a)(s-b)}{a b}}=\sqrt{\frac{(s-c)(s-a)}{c a}} ...[By \,\,half\,\, angle\,\, formulae] \end{aligned}\\ &\text {}\\ &\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow \frac{(s-a)(s-b)^2(s-c)}{a b^2 c}=\frac{(s-c)(s-a)}{c a} \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{(s-b)^2}{b^2}=1 \\ & \Rightarrow(s-b)^2=b^2 \\ & \Rightarrow s-b=b \\ & \Rightarrow s=2 b \end{aligned} \end{aligned} $$

content: Let $\mathrm{A} \equiv(0,0), \mathrm{B}(3,0), \mathrm{C}(0,-4)$ are vertices of $\triangle A B C$, then the co-ordinates of incentre of $\triangle \mathrm{ABC}$ is
explanation:
Let $\bar{a}=0 \hat{i}+0 \hat{j}, \bar{b}=3 \hat{i}, \bar{c}=-4 \hat{j}$

$$ \begin{aligned} & \overline{\mathrm{AB}}=3 \hat{\mathrm{i}}, \overline{\mathrm{AC}}=-4 \hat{\mathrm{j}}, \overline{\mathrm{BC}}=-4 \hat{\mathrm{j}}-3 \hat{\mathrm{i}} \\ & |\overline{\mathrm{AB}}|=3,|\overline{\mathrm{AC}}|=4,|\overline{\mathrm{BC}}|=5 \end{aligned} $$

If $H(\bar{h})$ is the incentre of $\triangle A B C$, then

$$ \begin{aligned} \overline{\mathrm{h}} & =\frac{|\overline{\mathrm{BC}}| \overline{\mathrm{a}}+|\overline{\mathrm{AC}}| \overline{\mathrm{b}}+|\overline{\mathrm{AB}}|^{-} \overline{\mathrm{c}}}{|\overline{\mathrm{BC}}|+|\overline{\mathrm{AC}}|+|\overline{\mathrm{AB}}|} \\ & =\frac{5(0 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+0 \hat{\mathrm{k}})+4(3 \hat{\mathrm{i}})+3(-4 \hat{\mathrm{j}})}{3+4+5} \\ & =\hat{\mathrm{i}}-\hat{\mathrm{j}} \\ \Rightarrow H & \equiv(1,-1) \end{aligned} $$

content: In a triangle ABC with usual notations if $b \sin C(b \cos C+c \cos B)=42$, then area of triangle $\mathrm{ABC}=$
explanation:
$$ \begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow b \sin C(b \cos C+c \cos B)=42 \\ & \Rightarrow b \sin C(a)=42 \quad \ldots \text { [Projection rule] } \\ & \Rightarrow a b \sin C=42 \end{aligned} $$

$$ \text { } \begin{aligned} Area \,\,\,of\,\,\, \triangle \mathrm{ABC} & =\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{ab} \sin \mathrm{C} \\ & =\frac{1}{2} \times 42=21 \text { sq. units } \end{aligned} $$

content: In a triangle with one of the angles $120^{\circ}$, the lengths of the sides form an A.P. If length of the greatest side is 7 m , then the area of the triangle is
explanation:
Let the sides of triangle be $a-d, a, a+d$

$\therefore \quad$ Greatest side $(\mathrm{c})=7 \mathrm{~m}$

Let the common difference be d

$\therefore \quad$ Other sides are $7-\mathrm{d}$ and $7-2 \mathrm{~d}$

Using cosine rule,

$$ \begin{aligned} & \cos C=\frac{a^2+b^2-c^2}{2 a b} \\ & \Rightarrow \cos 120^{\circ}=\frac{(7-2 d)^2+(7-d)^2-49}{2(7-2 d)(7-d)} \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{-1}{2}=\frac{49-42 d+5 d^2}{2\left(49-21 d+2 d^2\right)} \\ & \Rightarrow d^2-9 b+14=0 \\ & \Rightarrow d=7 \text { or } 2 \end{aligned} $$

But $\mathrm{d} \neq 7$

$$ \begin{array}{ll} \therefore & d=2 \mathrm{~cm} \\ \therefore & a=5 \mathrm{~cm} \text { sides are } 3 \mathrm{~cm} \text { and } 5 \mathrm{~cm} \\ & \text { Area of triangle }=\frac{1}{2} a b \sin C \end{array} $$

$$ \begin{aligned} &\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\, =\frac{1}{2} \times 3 \times 5 \times \sin 120^{\circ} \\& \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,=\frac{15}{2} \times \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \\ &\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\, =\frac{15 \sqrt{3}}{4} \mathrm{~cm}^2 \end{aligned} $$

content: In $\triangle \mathrm{ABC}$, with usual notations, if $\cos \frac{B}{2}=\sqrt{\frac{c+a}{2 a}}$, then $a^2=$
explanation: $$ \begin{aligned} & \cos \frac{B}{2}=\sqrt{\frac{s(s-b)}{c a}} \quad \ldots[\text { By half angle formula }] \\ & \Rightarrow \sqrt{\frac{c+a}{2 a}}=\sqrt{\frac{s(s-b)}{c a}} \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{c+a}{2 a}=\frac{s(s-b)}{c a} \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{c+a}{2 a}=\frac{\frac{a+b+c}{2}\left(\frac{a+b+c}{2}-b\right)}{c a} \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{c+a}{2 a}=\frac{(a+c+b)(a+c-b)}{4 a c} \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{c+a}{2 a}=\frac{(a+c)^2-b^2}{4 a c} \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{c+a}{2 a}=\frac{a^2+2 a c+c^2-b^2}{4 a c} \\ & \Rightarrow 2 c^2+2 a c=a^2+2 a c+c^2-b^2 \\ & \Rightarrow a^2=b^2+c^2 \end{aligned} $$

content: In a triangle ABC with usual notations, $\cot \frac{\mathrm{A}}{2}+\cot \frac{\mathrm{B}}{2}+\cot \frac{\mathrm{C}}{2}=$

options.content: $\frac{\mathrm{s}^2}{\Delta}$, where $\Delta$ is the area of the triangle ABC .
$\frac{s}{\Delta}$, where $\Delta$ is the area of the triangle ABC .
$\frac{\Delta}{\mathrm{s}}$, where $\Delta$ is the area of the triangle ABC .
$\Delta$, where $\Delta$ is the area of the triangle ABC .

explanation:
Using half angle formula,

$$ \begin{aligned} \tan \frac{A}{2} & =\sqrt{\frac{(s-b)(s-c)}{s(s-a)}} \\ & =\sqrt{\frac{s(s-a)(s-b)(s-c)}{s^2(s-a)^2}} \end{aligned} $$

$\therefore \quad \tan \frac{\mathrm{A}}{2}=\frac{\Delta}{\mathrm{s}(\mathrm{s}-\mathrm{a})}$, where $\Delta$ is area of triangle ABC

Similarly,

$$ \begin{aligned} & \tan \frac{B}{2}=\frac{\Delta}{s(s-b)}, \tan \frac{C}{2}=\frac{\Delta}{s(s-c)} \\ & \cot \frac{A}{2}+\cot \frac{B}{2}+\cot \frac{C}{2} \\ & =\frac{1}{\tan \frac{A}{2}}+\frac{1}{\tan \frac{B}{2}}+\frac{1}{\tan \frac{C}{2}} \\ & =\frac{s(s-a)}{\Delta}+\frac{s(s-b)}{\Delta}+\frac{s(s-c)}{\Delta} \\ & =\frac{s}{\Delta}[3 s-(a+b+c)] \\ & =\frac{s}{\Delta}(3 s-2 s)=\frac{s^2}{\Delta} \end{aligned} $$

content: With usual notations, in $\triangle \mathrm{ABC}$, the lengths of two sides are 10 cm and 9 cm respectively. If angles $\mathrm{A}, \mathrm{B}, \mathrm{C}$ are in A.P. then perimeter of $\triangle \mathrm{ABC}$ is
explanation: $$ \begin{aligned} & \angle A, \angle B, \angle C \text { are in A.P. } \\ & \Rightarrow 2 B=A+C \\ & \Rightarrow 3 B=A+B+C \\ & \Rightarrow 3 B=180^{\circ} \\ & \Rightarrow B=60^{\circ} \\ & \cos B=\frac{c^2+a^2-b^2}{2 c a} \\ & \Rightarrow \cos 60^{\circ}=\frac{c^2+10^2-9^2}{2 c(10)} \quad \ldots[\text { Let } a=10, b=9] \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{1}{2}=\frac{c^2+100-81}{20 c} \\ & \Rightarrow 10 c=c^2+19 \\ & \Rightarrow c^2-10 c+19=0 \\ & \Rightarrow c=\frac{10 \pm \sqrt{100-76}}{2}=\frac{10 \pm 2 \sqrt{6}}{2}=5 \pm \sqrt{6} \\ & \therefore \text { Perimeter }=10+9+5+\sqrt{6}=(24+\sqrt{6}) \mathrm{cm} \end{aligned} $$

content: In a triangle ABC , with usual notations, if $\mathrm{a}=5, \mathrm{~b}=4, \cos (\mathrm{~A}-\mathrm{B})=\frac{31}{32}$, then $\mathrm{c}=$
explanation: $$ \begin{aligned} &\begin{aligned} & \tan \left(\frac{A-B}{2}\right)=\sqrt{\frac{1-\cos (A-B)}{1+\cos (A-B)}} \doteq \sqrt{\frac{1-\left(\frac{31}{32}\right)}{1+\left(\frac{31}{32}\right)}} \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{a-b}{a+b} \cot \frac{C}{2}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{63}} \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{1}{9} \cot \frac{C}{2}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{63}} \\ & \Rightarrow \tan \frac{C}{2}=\frac{\sqrt{7}}{3} \\ & \text { Now, } \cos C=\frac{1-\tan ^2\left(\frac{C}{2}\right)}{1+\tan ^2\left(\frac{C}{2}\right)} \\ & \Rightarrow \cos C=\frac{1-\left(\frac{7}{9}\right)}{1+\left(\frac{7}{9}\right)}=\frac{1}{8} \end{aligned}\\ &\text { By using cosine rule, we get }\\ &\begin{aligned} & c^2=a^2+b^2-2 a b \cos C \\ & \Rightarrow c^2=25+16-40 \times \frac{1}{8}=36 \Rightarrow c=6 \end{aligned} \end{aligned} $$

content: In a triangle ABC with usual notations if, $\cot \frac{A}{2}=\frac{b+c}{a}$, then the triangle $A B C$ is
explanation:
$$ \begin{aligned} & \cot \frac{A}{2}=\frac{b+c}{a} \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{\cos \frac{A}{2}}{\sin \frac{A}{2}}=\frac{k(\sin B+\sin C)}{k \sin A} \quad \cdots[B y \sin e ~ r u l e] \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{\cos \frac{A}{2}}{\sin \frac{A}{2}}=\frac{2 \sin \left(\frac{B+C}{2}\right) \cos \left(\frac{B-C}{2}\right)}{2 \sin \frac{A}{2} \cos \frac{A}{2}} \end{aligned} $$

$$ \begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow \cos \frac{A}{2}=\frac{\sin \left(\frac{\pi}{2}-\frac{A}{2}\right) \cos \left(\frac{B-C}{2}\right)}{\cos \frac{A}{2}} \\ & \Rightarrow \cos \frac{A}{2}=\cos \left(\frac{B-C}{2}\right) \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{A}{2}=\frac{B-C}{2} \Rightarrow A=B-C \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(i)\end{aligned} $$

Now, $\mathrm{A}+\mathrm{B}+\mathrm{C}=\pi$

$\Rightarrow \mathrm{B}-\mathrm{C}+\mathrm{B}+\mathrm{C}=\pi\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,…[From (i)]$

$\Rightarrow 2 \mathrm{~B}=\pi \Rightarrow \mathrm{B}=\frac{\pi}{2}$

content: In a triangle ABC with usual notations, if $3 \mathrm{a}=\mathrm{b}+\mathrm{c}$, then $\cot \frac{\mathrm{B}}{2} \cdot \cot \frac{\mathrm{C}}{2}=$
explanation: $$ \begin{aligned} & s=\frac{a+b+c}{2}=\frac{a+3 a}{2}=2 a \\ & \begin{aligned} \cot \frac{B}{2} \cot \frac{C}{2} & =\sqrt{\frac{s(s-b)}{(s-a)(s-c)}} \sqrt{\frac{s(s-c)}{(s-a)(s-b)}} \\ & =\frac{s}{s-a}=\frac{2 a}{2 a-a}=2 \end{aligned} \end{aligned} $$

content: In $\triangle A B C$, with usual notations, if $\mathrm{a}^4+\mathrm{b}^4+\mathrm{c}^4-2 \mathrm{a}^2 \mathrm{c}^2-2 \mathrm{c}^2 \mathrm{~b}^2=0$, then $\angle \mathrm{C}=\ldots$
explanation:
$$ \begin{aligned} & \text { Given, } a^4+b^4+c^4-2 a^2 c^2-2 c^2 b^2=0 \\ & \Rightarrow a^4+b^4+c^4=2 a^2 c^2+2 c^2 b^2\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(i) \end{aligned} $$

Using cosine rule,

$$ \begin{aligned} & \cos C=\frac{a^2+b^2-c^2}{2 a b} \\ & \Rightarrow a^2+b^2-c^2=2 a b \cos C \end{aligned} $$

Squaring on both sides, we get

$$ \begin{aligned} a^4+b^4+c^4+2 a^2 b^2-2 b^2 c^2-2 a^2 c^2 & =4 a^2 b^2 \cos ^2 C \\ \Rightarrow 2 a^2 c^2+2 c^2 b^2+2 a^2 b^2-2 b^2 c^2-2 a^2 c^2 = & 4 a^2 b^2 \cos ^2 C \,\,\,\,\,\ldots[\text { From (i) }]\end{aligned} $$

$$ \begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow 2 a^2 b^2=4 a^2 b^2 \cos ^2 C \\ & \Rightarrow \cos ^2 C=\frac{1}{2} \\ & \Rightarrow \cos C=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \\ & \Rightarrow C=135^{\circ} \end{aligned} $$

content: In a triangle ABC with usual notations if, $\tan \left(\frac{\mathrm{B}-\mathrm{C}}{2}\right)=x \cot \frac{\mathrm{~A}}{2}$, then $x=$
explanation:
$$ \tan \left(\frac{\mathrm{B}-\mathrm{C}}{2}\right)=x \cot \frac{\mathrm{~A}}{2} $$

We have,

$$ \tan \left(\frac{\mathrm{B}-\mathrm{C}}{2}\right)=\left(\frac{\mathrm{b}-\mathrm{c}}{\mathrm{~b}+\mathrm{c}}\right) \cot \frac{\mathrm{A}}{2} $$

                                                    …[Napier's Analogies]

$$ \begin{aligned} \therefore \quad & \left(\frac{\mathrm{b}-\mathrm{c}}{\mathrm{~b}+\mathrm{c}}\right) \cot \frac{\mathrm{A}}{2}=x \cot \frac{\mathrm{~A}}{2} \\ & \Rightarrow x=\frac{\mathrm{b}-\mathrm{c}}{\mathrm{~b}+\mathrm{c}} \end{aligned} $$

content: With usual notations, the perimeter of a triangle ABC is 6 times the arithmetic mean of sine of its angles. If $\mathrm{a}=1$, then $\angle \mathrm{A}=$
explanation: $$ \begin{aligned} &\text { According to the given condition, }\\ &\begin{aligned} & a+b+c=\frac{6(\sin A+\sin B+\sin C)}{3} \\ & \Rightarrow k(\sin A+\sin B+\sin C) \\ & \quad=2(\sin A+\sin B+\sin C) \\ & \text { where } k=\frac{a}{\sin A}=\frac{b}{\sin B}=\frac{c}{\sin C} \\ & \Rightarrow k=2 \quad \ldots .[\because \sin A+\sin B+\sin C \neq 0] \\ & \therefore \quad \frac{a}{\sin A}=2 \Rightarrow \sin A=\frac{1}{2} \quad \ldots .[\because a=1] \\ & \Rightarrow A=\frac{\pi}{6} \end{aligned} \end{aligned} $$

content: In a triangle ABC , with usual notations, $\tan \left(\frac{\mathrm{A}}{2}\right)=\frac{5}{6}, \tan \left(\frac{\mathrm{C}}{2}\right)=\frac{2}{5}$, then
explanation:
We have,

$$ \begin{aligned} & \tan \left(\frac{A}{2}\right)=\sqrt{\frac{(s-b)(s-c)}{s(s-a)}}, \tan \left(\frac{C}{2}\right)=\sqrt{\frac{(s-a)(s-b)}{s(s-c)}} \\ & \Rightarrow \tan \left(\frac{A}{2}\right) \tan \left(\frac{C}{2}\right)=\sqrt{\frac{(s-b)(s-c)}{s(s-a)} \times \frac{(s-a)(s-b)}{s(s-c)}} \\ & \quad=\sqrt{\frac{(s-b)^2}{s^2}} \\ & \tan \left(\frac{A}{2}\right) \tan \left(\frac{C}{2}\right)=\frac{s-b}{s} \\ & \frac{5}{6} \times \frac{2}{5}=\frac{s-b}{s} \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\text { ...[Given] }\\ & \frac{1}{3}=\frac{s-b}{s} \\ & s=3 s-3 b \\ & 3 b=3 s-s \\ & \Rightarrow 2 s=3 b \\ & \Rightarrow s=\frac{3 b}{2} \end{aligned} $$

$$ \begin{aligned} &\text { we known that, }\\ &\begin{aligned} & s=\frac{a+b+c}{2} \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{3 b}{2}=\frac{a+b+c}{2} \\ & \Rightarrow 3 b=a+b+c \\ & \Rightarrow 2 b=a+c \\ & \Rightarrow a, b, c \text { are in } A . P \end{aligned} \end{aligned} $$

content: With usual notation, in triangle ABC , $\mathrm{m} \angle \mathrm{A}=30^{\circ}$ then the value of $\left(1+\frac{\mathrm{a}}{\mathrm{c}}+\frac{\mathrm{b}}{\mathrm{c}}\right)\left(1+\frac{\mathrm{c}}{\mathrm{b}}-\frac{\mathrm{a}}{\mathrm{b}}\right)$ is equal to
explanation: $$ \begin{aligned} &\begin{aligned} & \left(1+\frac{a}{c}+\frac{b}{c}\right)\left(1+\frac{c}{b}-\frac{a}{b}\right) \\ & =\left(\frac{c+a+b}{c}\right)\left(\frac{b+c-a}{b}\right) \\ & =\frac{1}{b c}\left[(b+c)^2-a^2\right] \\ & =\frac{1}{b c}\left(b^2+c^2+2 b c-a^2\right) \\ & =\frac{b^2+c^2-a^2}{b c}+2 \\ & =2\left(\frac{b^2+c^2-a^2}{2 b c}\right)+2 \\ & =2 \cos A+2 &\text { ...[By cosine rule] }\\ & =2 \cos 30^{\circ}+2 \\ & =\sqrt{3}+2 \end{aligned}\\ \end{aligned} $$

content: In $\triangle A B C$, with usual notations, $a \cos B=b \cos A, a \cos C \neq c \cos A$ then $\mathrm{A}(\triangle \mathrm{ABC})$ $\qquad$ sq. units.
explanation:
$$ \begin{aligned} & a \cos B=b \cos A \\ & \begin{aligned} \Rightarrow a\left(\frac{a^2+c^2-b^2}{2 a c}\right) & =b\left(\frac{b^2+c^2-a^2}{2 b c}\right) \\ \Rightarrow & \frac{a^2+c^2-b^2}{2 c}=\frac{b^2+c^2-a^2}{2 c} \\ \Rightarrow & a^2+c^2-b^2=b^2+c^2-a^2 \\ \Rightarrow & a^2-b^2=b^2-a^2 \\ \Rightarrow a & =b \\ \text { Area of triangle } & =\frac{1}{2} a b \sin C \\ & =\frac{1}{2} a^2 \sin C \quad \ldots[\because a=b] \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(i)\end{aligned} \end{aligned} $$

$$ \begin{aligned} &\text { By using Cosine Rule, }\\ &\begin{aligned} & c^2=a^2+b^2-2 a b \cos C \\ & \Rightarrow c^2=2 a^2-2 a^2 \cos C \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\ldots[\because a=b]\\ & \Rightarrow \cos C=\frac{2 a^2-c^2}{2 a^2} \\ & \sin ^2 C=1-\cos ^2 C \\ & \quad=1-\left(\frac{2 a^2-c^2}{2 a^2}\right)^2 \\ & \quad=1-\frac{\left(2 a^2-c^2\right)^2}{4 a^4} \\ & \quad=\frac{4 a^4-\left(4 a^4-4 a^2 c^2+c^4\right)}{4 a^4} \\ & \therefore \quad \sin { }^2 C=\frac{4 a^2 c^2-c^4}{4 a^4}=\frac{c^2\left(4 a^2-c^2\right)}{4 a^4} \\ & \therefore \quad \sin C=\frac{c \sqrt{4 a^2-c^2}}{2 a^2} \end{aligned} \end{aligned} $$

Area of triangle $=\frac{1}{2} a^2\left(\frac{c \sqrt{4 a^2-c^2}}{2 a^2}\right)…[From (i)]$

$$ =\frac{c \sqrt{4 a^2-c^2}}{4} $$

content: In a triangle ABC , with usual notations if $\mathrm{a}=4, \mathrm{~b}=8, \angle \mathrm{C}=60^{\circ}$, then the value of $\angle \mathrm{B}$ and the ratio $\cos \mathrm{A}: \cos \mathrm{C}$ respectively are,
explanation:
By using cosine rule, we get

$$ \begin{aligned} c^2 & =a^2+b^2-2 a b \cos C \\ & =4^2+8^2-2 \times 4 \times 8 \times \cos 60^{\circ} \\ & =16+64-64 \times \frac{1}{2} \\ \therefore \quad c^2 & =48 \\ \therefore \quad c & =\sqrt{48}=4 \sqrt{3} \end{aligned} $$

Now,

$$ \begin{aligned} \cos B & =\frac{a^2+c^2-b^2}{2 a c}=\frac{4^2+(4 \sqrt{3})^2-8^2}{2 \times 4 \times 4 \sqrt{3}} \\ & =\frac{16+48-64}{32 \sqrt{3}} \end{aligned} $$

$$ \begin{aligned} & \therefore \quad \cos B=0 \\ & \therefore \quad \angle \mathrm{~B}=\frac{\pi}{2} \\ & \cos \mathrm{~A}=\frac{\mathrm{b}^2+\mathrm{c}^2-\mathrm{a}^2}{2 \mathrm{bc}}=\frac{64+48-16}{2 \times 8 \times 4 \sqrt{3}} \\ & =\frac{96}{64 \sqrt{3}}=\frac{3}{2 \sqrt{3}}=\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \\ & \cos \mathrm{C}=\cos 60^{\circ}=\frac{1}{2} \\ & \therefore \quad \cos A: \cos C=\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}: \frac{1}{2}=\sqrt{3}: 1 \end{aligned} $$

content: In a triangle ABC with usual notations if $|\overline{\mathrm{BC}}|=8,|\overline{\mathrm{CA}}|=7,|\overline{\mathrm{AB}}|=10$ then the projection of $\overline{\mathrm{AB}}$ on $\overline{\mathrm{AC}}$ is
explanation:
Let $\overline{\mathrm{AB}}=\mathrm{c}=10, \overline{\mathrm{BC}}=\mathrm{a}=8, \overline{\mathrm{CA}}=\mathrm{b}=7$

By using cosine rule,

$$ \begin{aligned} \cos \mathrm{A} & =\frac{b^2+c^2-a^2}{2 b c} \\ & =\frac{7^2+10^2-8^2}{2 \times 7 \times 10} \\ & =\frac{85}{140} \\ & =\frac{17}{28} \\ \overline{\mathrm{AB}} \cdot \overline{\mathrm{AC}} & =|\overline{\mathrm{AB}}||\overline{\mathrm{AC}}| \cos \mathrm{A} \\ & =10 \times 7 \times \frac{17}{28} \\ & =\frac{85}{2} \end{aligned} $$

Projection of $\overline{\mathrm{AB}}$ on $\overline{\mathrm{AC}}=\frac{\overline{\mathrm{AB}} \cdot \overline{\mathrm{AC}}}{|\overline{\mathrm{AC}}|}$

$$ =\frac{\frac{85}{2}}{7}=\frac{85}{14} \text { units } $$

content:
In a triangle ABC , the sides $\mathrm{a}, \mathrm{b}, \mathrm{c}$ are such that they are the roots of the equation $x^3-11 x^2+38 x-40=0$ Then

$$ \frac{\cos A}{a}+\frac{\cos B}{b}+\frac{\cos C}{c}= $$
explanation:
Given, $x^3-11 x^2+38 x-40=0$

$$ \begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow(x-4)\left(x^2-7 x+10\right)=0 \\ & \Rightarrow(x-4)(x-5)(x-2)=0 \end{aligned} $$

$\mathrm{a}, \mathrm{b}, \mathrm{c}$ are the roots of the given equation.

$$ \Rightarrow a=4, b=5, c=2 $$

By using cosine rule, we get

$$ \begin{aligned} & \frac{\cos A}{a}+\frac{\cos B}{b}+\frac{\cos C}{c} \\ & =\frac{1}{a}\left(\frac{b^2+c^2-a^2}{2 b c}\right)+\frac{1}{b}\left(\frac{c^2+a^2-b^2}{2 c a}\right)+\frac{1}{c}\left(\frac{a^2+b^2-c^2}{2 a b}\right) \\ & =\frac{b^2+c^2-a^2+c^2+a^2-b^2+a^2+b^2-c^2}{2 a b c} \\ & =\frac{a^2+b^2+c^2}{2 a b c} \\ & =\frac{4^2+5^2+2^2}{2 \times 4 \times 5 \times 2} \\ & =\frac{45}{80}=\frac{9}{16} \end{aligned} $$

content: In a triangle ABC with usual notations if $\mathrm{a}=13$, $b=14, c=15$ Then $\sin A=$
explanation:
$$ \begin{aligned} s & =\frac{a+b+c}{2}=\frac{13+14+15}{2}=\frac{42}{2}=21 \\ \Delta & =\sqrt{s(s-a)(s-b)(s-c)} \\ & =\sqrt{21(21-13)(21-14)(21-15)} \\ & =\sqrt{21 \times 8 \times 7 \times 6} \\ & =\sqrt{7056} \\ & =84 \end{aligned} $$

$$ \begin{aligned} &\therefore \quad \sin A=\frac{2 \Delta}{b c}=\frac{2 \times 84}{14 \times 15}=\frac{4}{5}\\ &\text { Alternate method: }\\ &\begin{aligned} \cos A & =\frac{b^2+c^2-a^2}{2 b c} \\ & =\frac{(14)^2+(15)^2-(13)^2}{2(14)(15)} \\ & =\frac{196+225-169}{420} \\ & =\frac{252}{420}=\frac{21}{35} \\ \sin ^2 A & =1-\cos ^2 A=1-\left(\frac{21}{35}\right)^2 \\ & =1-\frac{441}{1225} \\ & =\frac{784}{1225} \\ \therefore \quad \sin A & =\frac{28}{35}=\frac{4}{5} \end{aligned} \end{aligned} $$

content: In a triangle $A B C$, with usual notations, $3 \mathrm{~b}=\mathrm{a}+\mathrm{c}$, then $\cot \frac{\mathrm{A}}{2} \cdot \cot \frac{\mathrm{C}}{2}=$
explanation:
$$ \begin{aligned} &\begin{aligned} & \cot \left(\frac{A}{2}\right) \cdot \cot \left(\frac{C}{2}\right) \\ & =\frac{1}{\tan \left(\frac{A}{2}\right) \cdot \tan \left(\frac{C}{2}\right)} \\ & =\sqrt{\frac{s(s-a)}{(s-b)(s-c)} \times \frac{s(s-c)}{(s-a)(s-b)}} \text { ...[By half angle formulae] }\end{aligned}\\ &=\sqrt{\frac{s^2}{(s-b)^2}} \end{aligned} $$

$$ \begin{aligned} &\begin{aligned} \therefore \quad & \cot \left(\frac{A}{2}\right) \cdot \cot \left(\frac{C}{2}\right)=\frac{s}{s-b} \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(i)\\ & 2 s=a+b+c \\ & \Rightarrow 2 s=3 b+b \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\ldots[a+c=3 b(\text { given })]\\ & \Rightarrow 2 s=4 b \\ & \Rightarrow s=2 b \end{aligned}\\ &\text { Substituting } s=2 b \text { in equation (i), we get }\\ &\cot \left(\frac{A}{2}\right) \cdot \cot \left(\frac{C}{2}\right)=\frac{2 b}{2 b-b}=\frac{2 b}{b}=2 \end{aligned} $$

content: In a triangle ABC , with usual notations if $\frac{2 \cos \mathrm{~A}}{\mathrm{a}}+\frac{\cos \mathrm{B}}{\mathrm{b}}+\frac{2 \cos \mathrm{C}}{\mathrm{c}}=\frac{\mathrm{a}}{\mathrm{bc}}+\frac{\mathrm{b}}{\mathrm{ca}}$ then $\angle \mathrm{A}=$
explanation:
$$ \begin{aligned} &\begin{aligned} \therefore & \frac{2 \cos A}{a}+\frac{\cos B}{b}+\frac{2 \cos C}{c}=\frac{a}{b c}+\frac{b}{a c} \\ \therefore & \frac{2\left(b^2+c^2-a^2\right)}{2 a b c}+\frac{a^2+c^2-b^2}{2 a b c} +\frac{2\left(a^2+b^2-c^2\right)}{2 a b c}=\frac{2 a^2+2 b^2}{2 a b c} ...[By \,\,cosine\,\, rule]\end{aligned}\text { } \end{aligned} $$

$$ \begin{aligned} \therefore \quad 2 b^2+2 c^2-2 a^2+a^2+c^2-b^2+2 a^2+ & 2 b^2-2 c^2 =2 a^2+2 b^2 \end{aligned} $$

$$ \begin{aligned} &\therefore \quad \mathrm{a}^2=\mathrm{b}^2+\mathrm{c}^2\\ &\therefore \quad \mathrm{ABC} \text { is a right angled triangle. }\\ &\therefore \quad \angle \mathrm{A}=\frac{\pi}{2} \end{aligned} $$

content: In a triangle ABC with usual notations, if $\mathrm{a}, \mathrm{b}, \mathrm{c}$ are in arithmetic progression, then, $\tan \frac{\mathrm{A}}{2} \cdot \tan \frac{\mathrm{C}}{2}=$
explanation:
Since $\mathrm{a}, \mathrm{b}, \mathrm{c}$ are in A.P.,

$$ \begin{aligned} & a+c=2 b \\ & s=\frac{a+b+c}{2}=\frac{3 b}{2} \\ & \tan \frac{A}{2} \tan \frac{C}{2}=\sqrt{\frac{(s-b)(s-c)}{s(s-a)}} \sqrt{\frac{(s-a)(s-b)}{s(s-c)}} …[By\,\,\, half\,\,\, angle \,\,\,formulae]\end{aligned} $$

$$ \begin{aligned} & =\frac{s-b}{s}=\frac{\frac{3 b}{2}-b}{\frac{3 b}{2}} \\ & =\frac{1}{3} \end{aligned} $$

content: With usual notations, in a triangle $A B C$, if $\theta$ is any real number, then $a \cos (B-\theta)+b \cos (A+\theta)$ is
explanation:
$$ \begin{aligned} a \cos (B-\theta) & +b \cos (A+\theta) \\ =a(\cos B \cos \theta & +\sin B \sin \theta) +b(\cos A \cos \theta-\sin A \sin \theta) \\ =a \cos B \cos \theta & +a \sin B \sin \theta +b \cos A \cos \theta-b \sin A \sin \theta \\ =\cos \theta(a \cos B & +b \cos A) +\sin \theta(a \sin B-b \sin A) \end{aligned} $$

$$ \begin{aligned} &\begin{aligned} =\cos \theta\left[\frac{a\left(a^2+c^2-b^2\right)}{2 a c}+\right. & \left.\frac{b\left(b^2+c^2-a^2\right)}{2 b c}\right] +\sin \theta(a b k-a b k) ...[By\,\, cosine \,\,\,and \,\,\,sine rule]\end{aligned}\\ &\text { }\\ &\begin{aligned} & =\cos \theta\left(\frac{a^2+c^2-b^2+b^2+c^2-a^2}{2 c}\right) \\ & =c \cos \theta \end{aligned} \end{aligned} $$

content: If two sides of a triangle are $\sqrt{3}-2$ and $\sqrt{3}+2$ units and their included angle is $60^{\circ}$, then the third side of the triangle is
explanation:
Let the two given sides of the triangle be $a = \sqrt{3} - 2$ and $b = \sqrt{3} + 2$. The angle between them is $C = 60^{\circ}$.

We use the Cosine rule to find the third side:

Cosine Rule Formula: $c^2 = a^2 + b^2 - 2ab \cos C$

Substitute the values:

$ c^2 = (\sqrt{3}-2)^2 + (\sqrt{3}+2)^2 - 2(\sqrt{3}-2)(\sqrt{3}+2)\cos 60^\circ $

Calculate each part:

  • $(\sqrt{3}-2)^2 = (\sqrt{3})^2 - 2 \cdot \sqrt{3} \cdot 2 + 2^2 = 3 - 4\sqrt{3} + 4 = 7 - 4\sqrt{3}$
  • $(\sqrt{3}+2)^2 = (\sqrt{3})^2 + 2 \cdot \sqrt{3} \cdot 2 + 2^2 = 3 + 4\sqrt{3} + 4 = 7 + 4\sqrt{3}$
  • $(\sqrt{3}-2)(\sqrt{3}+2) = (\sqrt{3})^2 - (2)^2 = 3 - 4 = -1$
  • $\cos 60^\circ = \frac{1}{2}$

Now put these values into the formula:

$ c^2 = (7 - 4\sqrt{3}) + (7 + 4\sqrt{3}) - 2 \times (-1) \times \frac{1}{2} $

The terms $ -4\sqrt{3} $ and $ +4\sqrt{3} $ cancel out, so: $ c^2 = 7 + 7 - (-1) = 14 + 1 = 15 $

Take the square root to get the third side:

$ c = \sqrt{15} $

So, the third side of the triangle is $\sqrt{15}$ units.

content: The ratios of sides in a triangle ABC are $5: 12: 13$ and its area is 270 . Then sides of the triangle are

$$\begin{aligned} &\therefore \quad \text { Area of right angled triangle }\\ &\begin{aligned} & =\frac{1}{2} \times \text { product of sides containing right angle } \\ & \Rightarrow 270=\frac{1}{2} \times 5 x \times 12 x \\ & \Rightarrow x^2=\frac{270 \times 2}{5 \times 12} \\ & \Rightarrow x^2=9\\ & \Rightarrow x=3 \end{aligned} \end{aligned}$$

$\therefore \quad$ The sides of triangle are 15, 36 and 39

content: The smallest angle of the triangle whose sides are $6+\sqrt{12}, \sqrt{48}, \sqrt{24}$ is
explanation: $$\begin{aligned} & \text { Let } a=6+\sqrt{12}, b=\sqrt{48}, c=\sqrt{24} \\ & \cos C=\frac{a^2+b^2-c^2}{2 a b} \\ & =\frac{(6+\sqrt{12})^2+(\sqrt{48})^2-(\sqrt{24})^2}{2 \times(6+\sqrt{12}) \times \sqrt{48}} \\ & =\frac{48+24 \sqrt{3}+48-24}{4(3+\sqrt{3}) \times 4 \sqrt{3}}=\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \\ & \therefore \quad C=\cos ^{-1} \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}=\frac{\pi}{6} \end{aligned}$$

content: In a triangle $A B C$, with usual notations, if $\frac{b+c}{11}=\frac{c+a}{12}=\frac{a+b}{13}$ Then $\cos \mathrm{A}: \cos \mathrm{B}: \cos \mathrm{C}$ is
explanation:
Let $\frac{b+c}{11}=\frac{c+a}{12}=\frac{a+b}{13}=k$

$$\begin{array}{ll} \therefore & b+c=11 k \quad\text{...... (i)}\\ & c+a=12 k \quad\text{...... (ii)}\\ & \text { and } a+b=13 k \quad\text{...... (iii)} \end{array}$$

$$\begin{array}{ll} & \text { From (i) }+ \text { (ii) }+ \text { (iii), } 2(a+b+c)=36 k \\ \therefore \quad & a+b+c=18 k\quad\text{..... (iv)} \end{array}$$

Now, (iv) - (i) gives, $\mathrm{a}=7 \mathrm{k}$

(iv) - (ii) gives, $\mathrm{b}=6 \mathrm{k}$

(iv) - (iii) gives, $\mathrm{c}=5 \mathrm{k}$

Now,

$$\begin{aligned} \cos C & =\frac{a^2+b^2-c^2}{2 a b}=\frac{(7 k)^2+(6 k)^2-(5 k)^2}{2 \times(7 k) \times(6 k)} \\ & =\frac{49 k^2+36 k^2-25 k^2}{84 k^2} \\ & =\frac{60 k^2}{84 k^2} \\ & =\frac{5}{7} \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} \cos B & =\frac{a^2+c^2-b^2}{2 a c} \\ & =\frac{(7 k)^2+(5 k)^2-(6 k)^2}{2 \times 7 k \times 5 k} \\ & =\frac{49 k^2+25 k^2-36 k^2}{70 k^2} \\ & =\frac{38}{70}=\frac{19}{35} \\ \cos A & =\frac{b^2+c^2-a^2}{2 b c} \end{aligned}$$

$=\frac{(6 \mathrm{k})^2+(5 \mathrm{k})^2-(7 \mathrm{k})^2}{2 \times 6 \mathrm{k} \times 5 \mathrm{k}}$

$$\begin{aligned} & =\frac{36 \mathrm{k}^2+25 \mathrm{k}^2-49 \mathrm{k}^2}{60 \mathrm{k}^2} \\ & =\frac{12 \mathrm{k}^2}{60 \mathrm{k}^2} \\ & =\frac{1}{5} \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} \therefore \quad \cos A: \cos B: \cos C & =\frac{1}{5}: \frac{19}{35}: \frac{5}{7} \\ & =7: 19: 25 \end{aligned}$$

content: In a triangle $\mathrm{ABC}, l(\mathrm{AB})=\sqrt{23}$ units, $l(\mathrm{BC})=3$ units, $l(\mathrm{CA})=4$ units, then $\frac{\cot A+\cot C}{\cot B}$ is
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} & \frac{\cot A+\cot C}{\cot B} \\ & =\frac{\frac{\cos A}{\sin A}+\frac{\cos C}{\sin C}}{\frac{\cos B}{\sin B}} \\ & =\frac{\frac{b^2+c^2-a^2}{2 b c \sin A}+\frac{a^2+b^2-c^2}{2 a b \sin C}}{\frac{c^2+a^2-b^2}{2 c a \sin B}} \\ & =\frac{\frac{b^2+c^2-a^2}{2(2 \Delta)}+\frac{a^2+b^2-c^2}{2(2 \Delta)}}{\frac{c^2+a^2-b^2}{2(2 \Delta)}} \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & =\frac{2 b^2}{c^2+a^2-b^2} \\ & =\frac{2(4)^2}{(\sqrt{23})^2+(3)^2-(4)^2} \\ & =\frac{32}{16} \\ & =2 \end{aligned}$$

content: In a triangle ABC , with usual notations, $2 \mathrm{ac} \sin \left(\frac{\mathrm{A}-\mathrm{B}+\mathrm{C}}{2}\right)$ is equal to
explanation: $$\begin{aligned} 2 \mathrm{ac} \sin \frac{\mathrm{~A}-\mathrm{B}+\mathrm{C}}{2} & =2 \mathrm{ac} \sin \frac{\pi-2 \mathrm{~B}}{2} \\ & =2 \mathrm{ac} \cos \mathrm{~B} \\ & =2 \mathrm{ac} \frac{\mathrm{c}^2+\mathrm{a}^2-\mathrm{b}^2}{2 \mathrm{ca}} \quad\text{.... [By cosine rule]}\\ & =\mathrm{c}^2+\mathrm{a}^2-\mathrm{b}^2 \end{aligned}$$

content: In a triangle ABC , with usual notations, $\frac{\cos \mathrm{B}+\cos \mathrm{C}}{\mathrm{b}+\mathrm{c}}+\frac{\cos \mathrm{A}}{\mathrm{a}}$ has the value
explanation: $$\begin{aligned} & \frac{\cos B+\cos C}{b+c}+\frac{\cos A}{a} \\ & =\frac{a \cos B+a \cos C+b \cos A+c \cos A}{a(b+c)} \\ & =\frac{(a \cos B+b \cos A)+(a \cos C+c \cos A)}{a(b+c)} \\ & =\frac{c+b}{a(b+c)} \quad\text{.... [By projection rule]}\\ & =\frac{1}{a} \end{aligned}$$

content: The angles of a triangle are in the ratio $5: 1: 6$, then ratio of the smallest side to the greatest side is
explanation:
Let the angles of the triangle be $5 x, x, 6 x$

$$\begin{array}{ll} \therefore & 5 x+x+6 x=180^{\circ} \\ \therefore & 12 x=180^{\circ} \\ \therefore & x=15^{\circ} \end{array}$$

$\therefore \quad$ Three angles are $75^{\circ}, 15^{\circ}, 90^{\circ}$.

$$\begin{aligned} & \frac{\sin 75^{\circ}}{\mathrm{a}}=\frac{\sin 15^{\circ}}{\mathrm{b}}=\frac{\sin 90^{\circ}}{\mathrm{c}}=\mathrm{k} \\ \therefore \quad & \frac{\mathrm{~b}}{\mathrm{c}}=\frac{\frac{\sin 15^{\circ}}{\mathrm{k}}}{\frac{\sin 90^{\circ}}{\mathrm{k}}}=\frac{\frac{\sqrt{3}-1}{2 \sqrt{2}}}{1} \end{aligned}$$

$$\therefore \quad \text { Required ratio }=\mathrm{b}: \mathrm{c}=\sqrt{3}-1: 2 \sqrt{2}$$

content: In a $\triangle \mathrm{PQR}, \mathrm{m} \angle \mathrm{R}=\frac{\pi}{2}$. If $\tan \left(\frac{\mathrm{P}}{2}\right)$ and $\tan \left(\frac{\mathrm{Q}}{2}\right)$ are the roots of the equation $a x^2+b x+c=0(a \neq 0)$, then
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} & \text { In } \triangle \mathrm{PQR}, \\ & \angle \mathrm{P}+\angle \mathrm{Q}+\angle \mathrm{R}=180^{\circ} \\ \therefore \quad & \angle \mathrm{P}+\angle \mathrm{Q}+\frac{\pi}{2}=180^{\circ} \\ \therefore \quad & \angle \mathrm{P}+\angle \mathrm{Q}=\frac{\pi}{2} \\ \therefore \quad & \frac{\angle \mathrm{P}}{2}+\frac{\angle \mathrm{Q}}{2}=\frac{\pi}{4} \end{aligned}$$

$\tan \left(\frac{\mathrm{P}}{2}\right)$ and $\tan \left(\frac{\mathrm{Q}}{2}\right)$ are roots of the equation $a x^2+b x+c=0\quad \text{..[Given]}$

$$\begin{aligned} \therefore \quad & \text { Sum of roots }=\frac{-b}{a} \\ & \tan \left(\frac{P}{2}\right)+\tan \left(\frac{Q}{2}\right)=\frac{-b}{a} \end{aligned}$$

Also, $\tan \left(\frac{\mathrm{P}}{2}\right) \cdot \tan \left(\frac{\mathrm{Q}}{2}\right)=\frac{\mathrm{c}}{\mathrm{a}}$

Using, $\tan \left(\frac{\mathrm{P}}{2}+\frac{\mathrm{Q}}{2}\right)=\frac{\tan \frac{\mathrm{P}}{2}+\tan \frac{\mathrm{Q}}{2}}{1-\tan \frac{\mathrm{P}}{2} \tan \frac{\mathrm{Q}}{2}}$, we get

$$\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow \tan \left(\frac{\pi}{4}\right)=\frac{\frac{-b}{a}}{1-\frac{c}{a}} \\ & \Rightarrow 1=\frac{-b}{a-c} \\ & \Rightarrow a-c=-b \\ & \Rightarrow a+b=c \end{aligned}$$

content: If the sides of a triangle $a, b, c$ are in A.P., then with usual notations, a $\cos ^2 \frac{\mathrm{C}}{2}+\mathrm{c} \cos ^2 \frac{\mathrm{~A}}{2}$ is
explanation: $$\begin{aligned} &\text { Since a, b, c are in A. P., }\\ &\begin{aligned} & 2 b=a+c \\ & a \cos ^2\left(\frac{C}{2}\right)+c^2 \cos ^2\left(\frac{A}{2}\right) \\ & =\frac{a(1+\cos C)}{2}+\frac{c(1+\cos A)}{2} \\ & =\frac{a+c+a \cos C+c \cos A}{2} \\ & =\frac{a+c+b}{2} \\ & =\frac{2 b+b}{2}=\frac{3 b}{2} \end{aligned} \end{aligned}$$

content: If in a triangle $A B C$, with usual notations, the angles are in A.P. and $b: c=\sqrt{3}: \sqrt{2}$, then angle $\mathrm{A}=$
explanation:
In a triangle $ABC$, if the angles are in arithmetic progression (A.P.), we can denote the angles as $A = \alpha - d$, $B = \alpha$, and $C = \alpha + d$.

Since the sum of the angles in any triangle is $180^\circ$, we have:

$$ (\alpha - d) + \alpha + (\alpha + d) = 180^\circ $$

This simplifies to:

$$ 3\alpha = 180^\circ $$

So:

$$ \alpha = 60^\circ $$

Therefore, the angles are $A = 60^\circ - d$, $B = 60^\circ$, and $C = 60^\circ + d$.

We are also given that the ratio of the sides is $b: c = \sqrt{3}: \sqrt{2}$. According to the law of sines, the sides can also be expressed in terms of their opposite angles:

$$ \frac{b}{c} = \frac{\sin B}{\sin C} = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{\sqrt{2}} $$

Using the angle values $B = 60^\circ$ and $C = 60^\circ + d$, we have:

$$ \frac{\sin 60^\circ}{\sin (60^\circ + d)} = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{\sqrt{2}} $$

The sine of $60^\circ$ is $\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}$. Therefore, we have:

$$ \frac{\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}}{\sin (60^\circ + d)} = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{\sqrt{2}} $$

This simplifies to:

$$ \sin (60^\circ + d) = \frac{\sqrt{2}}{2} $$

The sine value $\frac{\sqrt{2}}{2}$ corresponds to $45^\circ$. Hence:

$$ 60^\circ + d = 45^\circ $$

Solving for $d$ gives:

$$ d = -15^\circ $$

Substituting back to find angle $A$, we have:

$$ A = 60^\circ - d = 60^\circ + 15^\circ = 75^\circ $$

Thus, angle $A$ is $75^\circ$.

The correct answer is:

Option C: $75^\circ$

content: With usual notations, if the lengths of the sides of a triangle are $7 \mathrm{~cm}, 4 \sqrt{3} \mathrm{~cm}$ and $\sqrt{13} \mathrm{~cm}$, then the measures of the smallest angle is
explanation: $$\begin{aligned} & \text { Let } a=7, b=4 \sqrt{3}, c=\sqrt{13} \\ & \cos C=\frac{a^2+b^2-c^2}{2 a b}=\frac{49+48-13}{2 \times 7 \times 4 \sqrt{3}}=\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \\ & \therefore \quad \angle C=\frac{\pi}{6} \end{aligned}$$

content: In a triangle ABC , with usual notations, if $\mathrm{m} \angle \mathrm{A}=45^{\circ}, \mathrm{m} \angle B=75^{\circ}$, then $\mathrm{a}+\mathrm{c} \sqrt{2}$ has the
explanation:
Given that:

$$\mathrm{m} \angle \mathrm{~A}=45^{\circ}, \mathrm{m} \angle \mathrm{~B}=75^{\circ}, \mathrm{m} \angle \mathrm{C}=60^{\circ}$$

we know that

$$\begin{aligned} & \frac{\sin A}{a}=\frac{\sin B}{b}=\frac{\sin C}{c} \\ & \frac{\sin 45^{\circ}}{a}=\frac{\sin 75^{\circ}}{b}=\frac{\sin 60^{\circ}}{c} \\ \therefore \quad & \frac{\sin 45^{\circ}}{a}=\frac{\sin \left(45^{\circ}+30^{\circ}\right)}{b} \end{aligned}$$

$\therefore \quad \frac{1}{\sqrt{2} a}=\frac{\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \times \frac{1}{2}+\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \times \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}}{b}$

$$\begin{array}{ll} \therefore & \frac{1}{\sqrt{2} a}=\frac{1+\sqrt{3}}{2 \sqrt{2} b} \\ \therefore & a=\frac{2 b}{1+\sqrt{3}} \\ & \frac{\sin 60^{\circ}}{c}=\frac{\sin \left(45^{\circ}+30^{\circ}\right)}{b} \\ \therefore & \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2 c}=\frac{1+\sqrt{3}}{2 \sqrt{2} b} \\ \therefore & \sqrt{2} c=\frac{2 \sqrt{3} b}{(1+\sqrt{3})} \\ \therefore & a+\sqrt{2} c=\frac{2 b}{1+\sqrt{3}}+\frac{2 \sqrt{3} b}{1+\sqrt{3}}=2 b \end{array}$$

content: If the angles $\mathrm{A}, \mathrm{B}$ and C of a triangle ABC are in the ratio $2: 3: 7$ respectively, then the sides a, b and c are respectively in the ratio
explanation:
Angles of triangle ABC are in ratio $2: 3: 7$

Let the common multiple be $x$

$$\begin{array}{ll} \therefore & \angle A=2 x, \angle B=3 x, \angle C=7 x \\ \therefore & 2 x+3 x+7 x=180^{\circ} \end{array}$$

...[Sum of measure of angles of triangle is $180^{\circ}$ ]

$$\begin{array}{ll} \therefore & 12 x=180 \\ \therefore & x=15 \\ \therefore & \angle A \doteq 30^{\circ}, \angle B=45^{\circ}, \angle C=105^{\circ} \end{array}$$

Now, By sine Rule

$$\begin{aligned} & \frac{a}{\sin A}=\frac{b}{\sin B}=\frac{c}{\sin C} \\ & \frac{a}{\sin 30^{\circ}}=\frac{b}{\sin 45^{\circ}}=\frac{c}{\sin 105^{\circ}} \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \frac{a}{\frac{1}{2}}=\frac{b}{\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}}=\frac{c}{\frac{\sqrt{3}+1}{2 \sqrt{2}}} \\ & \frac{a}{\sqrt{2}}=\frac{b}{2}=\frac{c}{\sqrt{3}+1} \\ \therefore \quad & a: b=\sqrt{2}: 2: \sqrt{3}+1 \end{aligned}$$

content: If $\mathrm{A}, \mathrm{B}, \mathrm{C}$ are the angles of a triangle with $\tan \frac{A}{2}=\frac{1}{3}, \tan \frac{B}{2}=\frac{2}{3}$ then the value of $\tan \frac{C}{2}$ is
explanation:
In any triangle ABC, the sum of angles is always 180 degrees. Therefore, we have:

$$A + B + C = 180^{\circ}$$

Dividing by 2, we get:

$$\frac{A}{2} + \frac{B}{2} + \frac{C}{2} = 90^{\circ}$$

We can rearrange this equation to solve for $\frac{C}{2}$:

$$\frac{C}{2} = 90^{\circ} - \frac{A}{2} - \frac{B}{2}$$

Now, let's use the tangent addition formula:

$$\tan(x + y) = \frac{\tan x + \tan y}{1 - \tan x \tan y}$$

Applying this to our equation for $\frac{C}{2}$:

$$\tan\left(\frac{C}{2}\right) = \tan\left(90^{\circ} - \left(\frac{A}{2} + \frac{B}{2}\right)\right) = \cot\left(\frac{A}{2} + \frac{B}{2}\right)$$

Since $\cot(x) = \frac{1}{\tan(x)}$, we have:

$$\tan\left(\frac{C}{2}\right) = \frac{1}{\tan\left(\frac{A}{2} + \frac{B}{2}\right)}$$

Using the tangent addition formula again:

$$\tan\left(\frac{A}{2} + \frac{B}{2}\right) = \frac{\tan\left(\frac{A}{2}\right) + \tan\left(\frac{B}{2}\right)}{1 - \tan\left(\frac{A}{2}\right)\tan\left(\frac{B}{2}\right)}$$

Given that $\tan\left(\frac{A}{2}\right) = \frac{1}{3}$ and $\tan\left(\frac{B}{2}\right) = \frac{2}{3}$, we substitute these values:

$$\tan\left(\frac{A}{2} + \frac{B}{2}\right) = \frac{\frac{1}{3} + \frac{2}{3}}{1 - \left(\frac{1}{3}\right)\left(\frac{2}{3}\right)} = \frac{1}{1 - \frac{2}{9}} = \frac{1}{\frac{7}{9}} = \frac{9}{7}$$

Therefore:

$$\tan\left(\frac{C}{2}\right) = \frac{1}{\frac{9}{7}} = \frac{7}{9}$$

So the value of $\tan(\frac{C}{2})$ is $\frac{7}{9}$. The correct option is B.

content: The sides of a triangle are $\sin \theta, \cos \theta$ and $\sqrt{1+\sin \theta \cos \theta}$ for some $0<\theta<\frac{\pi}{2}$, then the greatest angle of a triangle is
explanation:
$$\mathrm{a}=\sin \theta, \mathrm{b}=\cos \theta \text { and } \mathrm{c}=\sqrt{1+\sin \theta \cos \theta}$$

Since $\sqrt{1+\sin \theta \cos \theta}$ is greater than $\sin \theta$ and $\cos \theta$.

$\therefore \quad \mathrm{C}$ is the greatest angle,

$$\begin{aligned} \therefore \quad \cos C & =\frac{a^2+b^2-c^2}{2 a b} \\ & =\frac{\sin ^2 \theta+\cos ^2 \theta-(1+\sin \theta \cos \theta)}{2 \sin \theta \cos \theta} \\ & =-\frac{1}{2}=\cos 120^{\circ} \\ \therefore \quad C= & 120^{\circ}=\frac{2 \pi}{3} \end{aligned}$$

content: For the triangle ABC , with usual notations, if the angles $A, B, C$ are in A.P. and $\mathrm{m} \angle \mathrm{A}=30^{\circ}, \mathrm{c}=3$, then the values of a and b are respectively
explanation:
Angles A, B, C are in A.P.

$$\begin{array}{ll} \therefore \quad & \angle \mathrm{A}+\angle \mathrm{C}=2 \angle \mathrm{~B} \\ & \text { Also, } \angle \mathrm{A}+\angle \mathrm{B}+\angle \mathrm{C}=180^{\circ} \\ & 2 \angle \mathrm{~B}+\angle \mathrm{B}=180^{\circ} \\ \therefore & \angle \mathrm{B}=60^{\circ} \\ & \angle \mathrm{A}=30^{\circ}\quad\text{... [Given]} \end{array}$$

$$\therefore \quad \angle C=90^{\circ}$$

Using sine Rule

$$\begin{aligned} & \frac{a}{\sin A}=\frac{b}{\sin B}=\frac{c}{\sin C} \\ \therefore & \frac{a}{\sin 30}=\frac{b}{\sin 60}=\frac{3}{\sin 90} \\ \therefore & \frac{a}{\frac{1}{2}}=\frac{b}{\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}}=\frac{3}{1} \\ & \Rightarrow 2 a=3, \frac{2 b}{\sqrt{3}}=3 \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow \mathrm{a}=\frac{3}{2} \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{~b}=\frac{3 \sqrt{3}}{2} \end{aligned}$$

content: If $(a+b) \cos C+(b+c) \cos A+(c+a) \cos B=72$ and if $a=18, b=24$, then area of the triangle $A B C$ is
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} \quad(a+b) \cos C+(b+c) & \cos A+(c+a) \cos B=72 \\ \therefore \quad a \cos C+b \cos C+b \cos A & +c \cos A \\ & +c \cos B+a \cos B=72 \\ \Rightarrow a \cos C+c \cos A+b & \cos A+a \cos B \\ & +b \cos C+c \cos B=72 \end{aligned}$$

$$\cdots\left[\begin{array}{l} \text { By projection } \\ a=b \cos C+c \cos B \\ b=c \cos A+a \cos C \\ c=a \cos B+b \cos A \end{array}\right]$$

$$\begin{aligned} &\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow b+c+a=72 \\ & \Rightarrow a+b+c=72 \\ & \Rightarrow 18+24+c=72 \quad[a=18, b=24 \ldots . \text { Given }] \\ & \Rightarrow c=30 \end{aligned}\\ &\begin{aligned} \text { Area of } \triangle \mathrm{ABC} & =\sqrt{\mathrm{s}(\mathrm{~s}-\mathrm{a})(\mathrm{s}-\mathrm{b})(\mathrm{s}-\mathrm{c})} \\ & =\sqrt{36(36-18)(36-24)(36-30)} \\ & =\sqrt{36 \times 18 \times 12 \times 6} \\ & =216 \text { sq. units } \end{aligned} \end{aligned}$$

content: If the angles of a triangle are in the ratio $4: 1: 1$, then the ratio of the longest side to the perimeter is
explanation:
Let the angles of the triangle be $4 x, x$ and $x$.

$\therefore 4 x+x+x=180^{\circ} \Rightarrow 6 x=180^{\circ} \Rightarrow x=30^{\circ}$

$\frac{\sin 120^{\circ}}{\mathrm{a}}=\frac{\sin 30^{\circ}}{\mathrm{b}}=\frac{\sin 30^{\circ}}{\mathrm{c}}$

$$\begin{aligned} \therefore \quad a & :(a+b+c) \\ & =\left(\sin 120^{\circ}\right):\left(\sin 120^{\circ}+\sin 30^{\circ}+\sin 30^{\circ}\right) \\ & =\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}: \frac{\sqrt{3}+2}{2}=\sqrt{3}: \sqrt{3}+2 \end{aligned}$$

content: In $\triangle \mathrm{ABC}$, with usual notations, if $\mathrm{b}=3$, $c=8, \mathrm{~m} \angle \mathrm{~A}=60^{\circ}$, then the circumradius of the triangle is _______ units.
explanation:
To find the circumradius $ R $ of triangle $ \triangle ABC $, we can use the formula:

$$ R = \frac{abc}{4K} $$

where $ K $ is the area of the triangle. However, a more straightforward approach when one angle is known is using:

$$ R = \frac{a}{2\sin A} $$

In this case:

Given $ b = 3 $, $ c = 8 $, and $ A = 60^\circ $.

First, apply the Law of Cosines to find $ a $:

$$ a^2 = b^2 + c^2 - 2bc \cos A $$

Substituting the known values:

$$ a^2 = 3^2 + 8^2 - 2 \cdot 3 \cdot 8 \cdot \cos 60^\circ $$

Since $ \cos 60^\circ = \frac{1}{2} $:

$$ a^2 = 9 + 64 - 2 \cdot 3 \cdot 8 \cdot \frac{1}{2} $$

$$ a^2 = 9 + 64 - 24 $$

$$ a^2 = 49 $$

$$ a = 7 $$

Now, substitute $ a $ and $ \sin A $ into the circumradius formula $ R = \frac{a}{2\sin A} $:

Since $ \sin 60^\circ = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} $:

$$ R = \frac{7}{2 \cdot \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}} $$

$$ R = \frac{7}{\sqrt{3}} $$

Thus, the circumradius of the triangle is:

Option C: $\frac{7}{\sqrt{3}}$.

content: If the lengths of the sides of triangle are 3,5,7, then the largest angle of the triangle is
explanation: $$\begin{aligned} & \text { Let } a=3, b=5, \mathrm{c}=7 \\ & \cos C=\frac{a^2+b^2-c^2}{2 a b}=\frac{9+25-49}{2 \times 3 \times 5}=\frac{-15}{30}=-\frac{1}{2} \\ \therefore \quad & \angle C=\frac{2 \pi}{3} \end{aligned}$$

content: In $\triangle A B C$, with usual notations, if $\frac{1}{b+c}+\frac{1}{c+a}=\frac{3}{a+b+c}$, then $m \angle C$ is equal to
explanation: $$\begin{aligned} &\begin{aligned} & \frac{1}{b+c}+\frac{1}{c+a}=\frac{3}{a+b+c} \\ & \frac{a+b+c}{b+c}+\frac{a+b+c}{c+a}=\frac{3(a+b+c)}{a+b+c} \\ & \frac{a}{b+c}+1+\frac{b}{c+a}+1=3 \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{a}{b+c}+\frac{b}{c+a}=1 \\ & \Rightarrow a(c+a)+b(b+c)=(b+c)(c+a) \\ & \Rightarrow a c+a^2+b^2+b c=b c+a b+c^2+a c \\ & \Rightarrow a^2+b^2-c^2=a b\quad\text{.... (i)} \end{aligned}\\ &\therefore \quad \text { By cosine Rule, }\\ &\begin{aligned} & \cos C=\frac{a^2+b^2-c^2}{2 a b} \\ & \cos C=\frac{a b}{2 a b} \quad \text{... [From (i)]}\\ & \Rightarrow \cos C=\frac{1}{2} \\ & \Rightarrow C=\frac{\pi}{3} \end{aligned} \end{aligned}$$

content: In a triangle $$\mathrm{A B C, m \angle A, m \angle B, m \angle C}$$ are in A.P. and lengths of two larger sides are 10 units, 9 units respectively, then the length (in units) of the third side is
explanation:
$$\angle \mathrm{A}, \angle \mathrm{B}, \angle \mathrm{C}$$ are in A.P.

$$\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow 2 B=A+C \\ & \Rightarrow 3 B=A+B+C \\ & \Rightarrow 3 B=180^{\circ} \\ & \Rightarrow B=60^{\circ} \\ & \cos B=\frac{c^2+a^2-b^2}{2 c a} \\ & \Rightarrow \cos 60^{\circ}=\frac{c^2+10^2-9^2}{2 c(10)} \quad \text{...[Let a = 10, b = 9]} \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow \frac{1}{2}=\frac{c^2+100-81}{20 c} \\ & \Rightarrow 10 c=c^2+19 \\ & \Rightarrow c^2-10 c+19=0 \\ & \Rightarrow c=\frac{10 \pm \sqrt{100-76}}{2} \\ & =\frac{10 \pm 2 \sqrt{6}}{2} \\ & =5 \pm \sqrt{6} \end{aligned}$$

content: In $$\triangle \mathrm{ABC}$$, with usual notations, $$2 \mathrm{ac} \sin \left(\frac{1}{2}(\mathrm{~A}-\mathrm{B}+\mathrm{C})\right)$$ is equal to
explanation: $$\begin{aligned} 2 \mathrm{ac} \sin \frac{\mathrm{A}-\mathrm{B}+\mathrm{C}}{2}= & 2 \mathrm{ac} \sin \frac{\pi-2 \mathrm{~B}}{2} \\ & =2 \mathrm{ac} \cos \mathrm{B} \\ & =2 \mathrm{ac} \frac{\mathrm{c}^2+\mathrm{a}^2-\mathrm{b}^2}{2 \mathrm{ca}} \quad \text{...[By cosine rule]}\\ & =\mathrm{c}^2+\mathrm{a}^2-\mathrm{b}^2 \end{aligned}$$

content: If the angles $$\mathrm{A}, \mathrm{B}$$, and $$\mathrm{C}$$ of a triangle are in an Arithmetic Progression and if $$\mathrm{a}, \mathrm{b}$$ and $$\mathrm{c}$$ denote the lengths of the sides opposite to A, B and C respectively, then the value of the expression $$\frac{\mathrm{a}}{\mathrm{c}} \sin 2 \mathrm{C}+\frac{\mathrm{c}}{\mathrm{a}} \sin 2 \mathrm{~A}$$ is
explanation:
A, B, C are in A.P.

$$\begin{array}{ll} \therefore \quad & \mathrm{A}+\mathrm{C}=2 \mathrm{~B} \\ & \mathrm{Also}, \mathrm{A}+\mathrm{B}+\mathrm{C}=180^{\circ} \\ \therefore \quad & \quad \mathrm{B}=60^{\circ} \end{array}$$

By sine rule,

$$\begin{aligned} & \frac{\sin A}{a}=\frac{\sin B}{b}=\frac{\sin C}{c}=k \\ \therefore \quad & \sin A=a k, \sin B=b k, \sin C=c k \\ \therefore \quad & \frac{a}{c} \sin 2 C+\frac{c}{a} \sin 2 A \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} \therefore \quad & \frac{a}{c} \sin 2 C+\frac{c}{a} \sin 2 A \\ & =\frac{a}{c}(2 \sin C \cos C)+\frac{c}{a}(2 \sin A \cos A) \\ & =\frac{a}{c}(2 c k \cos C)+\frac{c}{a}(2 a k \cos A) \\ & =2 k a \cos C+2 k c \cos A \\ & =2 k(a \cos C+c \cos A) \end{aligned}$$

$$=2 \mathrm{~kb} \quad \ldots[\because \mathrm{b}=\mathrm{a} \cos \mathrm{C}+\mathrm{c} \cos \mathrm{A}]$$

$$\begin{aligned} & =2 \sin \mathrm{B} \\ & =2 \times \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \quad \ldots .\left[\because \angle \mathrm{B}=60^{\circ}\right] \\ & =\sqrt{3} \end{aligned}$$

content: In $$\triangle A B C$$, with usual notations, if $$\frac{b+c}{11}=\frac{c+a}{12}=\frac{a+b}{13}$$, then the value of $$\cos A+\cos B+\cos C$$ is
explanation:
$$\frac{b+c}{11}=\frac{c+a}{12}=\frac{a+b}{13}=\frac{2(a+b+c)}{36}$$

or $$\frac{b+c}{11}=\frac{c+a}{12}=\frac{a+b}{13}=\frac{a+b+c}{18}=k$$

$$\therefore \quad b+c=11 k, c+a=12 k, a+b=13 k$$

$$a+b+c=18 k$$

Substituting $$b+c=11 k$$ in $$a+b+c=18 k$$, we get $$a+11 k=18 k \text { or } a=7 k$$

Substituting $$a=7 k$$ in $$c+a=12 k$$, we get

$$c+7 k=12 k \text { or } c=5 k$$

Substituting $$c=5 k$$ in $$b+c=11 k$$, we get

$$b+5 k=11 k$$ or $$b=6 k$$

$$\therefore \quad a=7 k, b=6 k$$ and $$c=5 k$$

Using cosine rule, we get

$$\begin{aligned} \cos A & =\frac{b^2+c^2-a^2}{2 b c}=\frac{(6 k)^2+(5 k)^2-(7 k)^2}{2 \times 6 k \times 5 k}=\frac{1}{5}, \\ \cos B & =\frac{c^2+a^2-b^2}{2 c a} \\ & =\frac{(5 k)^2+(7 k)^2-(6 k)^2}{2 \times 5 k \times 7 k}=\frac{19}{35} \\ \cos C & =\frac{a^2+b^2-c^2}{2 a b}=\frac{(7 k)^2+(6 k)^2-(5 k)^2}{2 \times 7 k \times 6 k}=\frac{5}{7} \\ \cos A & +\cos B+\cos C=\frac{1}{5}+\frac{19}{35}+\frac{5}{7} \\ & =\frac{7+19+25}{35}=\frac{51}{35} \end{aligned}$$

content: The sides of a triangle are three consecutive natural numbers and its largest angle is twice the smallest one, then the sides of the triangle (in units) are
explanation:
Let $$\mathrm{AC}=\mathrm{n}, \mathrm{AB}=\mathrm{n}+1, \mathrm{BC}=\mathrm{n}+2$$

$$\therefore \quad$$ Largest angle is $$\mathrm{A}$$ and smallest angle is $$\mathrm{B}$$.

$$\begin{array}{ll} \therefore \quad & A=2 B \\\\ & \text { Since } A+B+C=180^{\circ} \\\\ \therefore \quad & 3 B+C=180^{\circ} \\\\ \Rightarrow & C=180^{\circ}-3 B \\\\ \Rightarrow & \sin C=\sin \left(180^{\circ}-3 B\right)=\sin 3 B \end{array}$$

By sine rule,

$$\begin{aligned} & \frac{\sin A}{n+2}=\frac{\sin B}{n}=\frac{\sin C}{n+1} \\\\ & \frac{\sin 2 B}{n+2}=\frac{\sin B}{n}=\frac{\sin 3 B}{n+1} \\\\ & \Rightarrow \frac{2 \sin B \cos B}{n+2}=\frac{\sin B}{n}=\frac{3 \sin B-4 \sin ^3 B}{n+1} \\\\ & \frac{2 \cos B}{n+2}=\frac{1}{n}=\frac{3-4 \sin ^2 B}{n+1} \\\\ \therefore & \quad\cos B=\frac{n+2}{2 n}, 3-4 \sin ^2 B=\frac{n+1}{n} \\\\ \therefore & \quad 3-4\left(1-\cos ^2 B\right)=\frac{n+1}{n} \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \therefore \quad 3-4+4\left(\frac{n+2}{2 n}\right)^2=\frac{n+1}{n} \\\\ & \Rightarrow-1+\frac{n^2+4 n+4}{n^2}=\frac{n+1}{n} \\\\ & \Rightarrow-n^2+n^2+4 n+4=n^2+n \\\\ & \Rightarrow n^2-3 n-4=0 \\\\ & \Rightarrow(n+1)(n-4)=0 \\\\ & \Rightarrow n=-1 \text { or } n=4 \end{aligned}$$

But $$\mathrm{n}$$ cannot be negative.

$$\therefore\mathrm{n}=4$$

$$\therefore$$ The sides of the $$\Delta$$ are $$4,5,6$$.

content: In $$\triangle A B C$$ with usual notation, $$\frac{\cos A}{a}=\frac{\cos B}{b}=\frac{\cos C}{c}$$ and $$a=\frac{1}{\sqrt{6}}$$, then the area of triangle is _______ sq. units.
explanation:
If $$\frac{\cos A}{a}=\frac{\cos B}{b}=\frac{\cos C}{c}$$, then the triangle is equilateral.

$$\begin{aligned} \therefore \quad \mathrm{A}(\triangle \mathrm{ABC}) & =\frac{\sqrt{3}}{4} \mathrm{a}^2 \\ & =\frac{\sqrt{3}}{4}\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{6}}\right)^2 \\ & =\frac{\sqrt{3}}{24}=\frac{1}{8 \sqrt{3}} \text { sq. units } \end{aligned}$$

content: Angles of a triangle are in the ratio $$4: 1: 1$$. Then the ratio of its greatest side to its perimeter is
explanation:
Let the angles of the triangle be $$4 x, x$$ and $$x$$.

$$\therefore 4 x+x+x=180^{\circ} \Rightarrow 6 x=180^{\circ} \Rightarrow x=30^{\circ}$$

By sine rule,

$$\begin{aligned} & \frac{\sin 120^{\circ}}{\mathrm{a}}=\frac{\sin 30^{\circ}}{\mathrm{b}}=\frac{\sin 30^{\circ}}{\mathrm{c}} \\ & \therefore \quad \mathrm{a}:(\mathrm{a}+\mathrm{b}+\mathrm{c}) \\ &=\left(\sin 120^{\circ}\right):\left(\sin 120^{\circ}+\sin 30^{\circ}+\sin 30^{\circ}\right) \\ &=\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}: \frac{\sqrt{3}+2}{2}=\sqrt{3}: \sqrt{3}+2 \end{aligned}$$

content: The lengths of sides of a triangle are three consecutive natural numbers and its largest angle is twice the smallest one. Then the length of the sides of the triangle (in units) are
explanation:
Let $$a, a+1, a+2$$ be the sides of the triangle and $$A, B, C$$ be the angles opposite to them respectively.

According to the given condition, $$\mathrm{C}=2 \mathrm{~A}$$

$$\begin{aligned} \therefore \quad \sin C & =\sin 2 A \\ \sin C & =2 \sin A \cos A \quad \text{.... (i)} \end{aligned}$$

Note that $$\frac{\sin A}{a}=\frac{\sin C}{a+2}=k$$

$$\Rightarrow \sin \mathrm{A}=\mathrm{ka} \text { and } \sin \mathrm{C}=\mathrm{k}(\mathrm{a}+2)$$

$$\begin{aligned} \text { Also, } \cos A & =\frac{(a+1)^2+(a+2)^2-a^2}{2(a+1)(a+2)} \\ & =\frac{a^2+2 a+1+a^2+4 a+4-a^2}{2\left(a^2+3 a+2\right)} \\ & =\frac{a^2+6 a+5}{2\left(a^2+3 a+2\right)} \end{aligned}$$

$$\therefore \quad$$ (i) $$\Rightarrow k(a+2)=2 \times k a \times \frac{a^2+6 a+5}{2\left(a^2+3 a+2\right)}$$

$$\therefore \quad a+2=\frac{a\left(a^2+6 a+5\right)}{\left(a^2+3 a+2\right)}$$

$$\therefore \quad(a+2)\left(a^2+3 a+2\right)=a^3+6 a^2+5 a$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \therefore \quad a^3+5 a^2+8 a+4=a^3+6 a^2+5 a \\ & \therefore \quad a^2-3 a-4=0 \\ & \therefore \quad(a-4)(a+1)=0 \\ & \quad \Rightarrow a=4 \text { or }-1 \end{aligned}$$

But $$\mathrm{a}=-1$$ is not possible.

$$\therefore 4,5,6$$ are the lengths of the sides of the triangle.

content: If two angles of $$\triangle \mathrm{ABC}$$ are $$\frac{\pi}{4}$$ and $$\frac{\pi}{3}$$, then the ratio of the smallest and greatest sides are
explanation:
Let three angles of the triangle be given as $$\mathrm{A}=\frac{\pi}{4}, \mathrm{~B}=\frac{\pi}{3}$$ and $$\mathrm{C}=\frac{\pi}{4}+\frac{\pi}{6}$$

Let a, b, c be the opposite to angles A, B, C respectively.

As $$\frac{\sin A}{a}=\frac{\sin C}{c}$$, we get

Required Ratio $$=\frac{a}{c}=\frac{\sin A}{\sin C}$$

$$=\frac{\sin \left(\frac{\pi}{4}\right)}{\sin \left(\frac{\pi}{4}+\frac{\pi}{6}\right)}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & =\frac{\sin \frac{\pi}{4}}{\sin \frac{\pi}{4} \cos \frac{\pi}{6}+\cos \frac{\pi}{4} \sin \frac{\pi}{6}} \\ & =\frac{2}{\sqrt{3}+1} \\ & =\frac{2(\sqrt{3}-1)}{2} \\ & =(\sqrt{3}-1): 1 \end{aligned}$$

content: In $$\triangle \mathrm{ABC}, \mathrm{m} \angle \mathrm{B}=\frac{\pi}{3}$$ and $$\mathrm{m} \angle \mathrm{C}=\frac{\pi}{4}$$. Let point $$\mathrm{D}$$ divide $$\mathrm{BC}$$ internally in the ratio $$1: 3$$, then $$\frac{\sin (\angle B A D)}{\sin (\angle C A D)}$$ has the value
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} & \text { In } \triangle A B D \text {, } \\ & \frac{\sin (\angle B A D)}{B D}=\frac{\sin (\angle A B D)}{A D} \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{\sin (\angle \mathrm{BAD})}{x}=\frac{\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}}{\mathrm{AD}} \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{AD}=\frac{\sqrt{3} x}{2 \sin (\angle \mathrm{BAD})} \quad \text{.... (i)}\\ & \text { In } \triangle \mathrm{ADC} \text {, } \\ & \frac{\sin (\angle C A D)}{D C}=\frac{\sin (\angle A C D)}{A D} \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{\sin (\angle C A D)}{3 x}=\frac{\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}}{\mathrm{AD}} \\ & \therefore \quad \mathrm{AD}=\frac{3 x}{\sqrt{2} \sin (\angle \mathrm{CAD})} \quad \text{.... (ii)}\\ \end{aligned}$$

From (i) and (ii), we get

$$\begin{aligned} & \frac{\sqrt{3} x}{2 \sin (\angle \mathrm{BAD})}=\frac{3 x}{\sqrt{2} \sin (\angle \mathrm{CAD})} \\ \therefore \quad & \frac{\sin (\angle \mathrm{BAD})}{\sin (\angle \mathrm{CAD})}=\frac{\sqrt{6}}{6}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{6}} \end{aligned}$$

content: In a triangle, the sum of lengths of two sides is $$x$$ and the product of the lengths of the same two sides is $$y$$. If $$x^2-\mathrm{c}^2=y$$, where $$\mathrm{c}$$ is the length of the third side of the triangle, then the circumradius of the triangle is
explanation:
Let $$\mathrm{a}$$ and $$\mathrm{b}$$ be the lengths of two sides of $$\mathrm{a}$$ triangle.

$$\therefore \quad$$ According to the given condition,

$$\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{a}+\mathrm{b}=x \text { and } \mathrm{ab}=y \\ & \therefore \quad x^2-c^2=y \Rightarrow(a+b)^2-c^2=a b \\ & \Rightarrow a^2+b^2+2 a b-c^2=a b \\ & \Rightarrow a^2+b^2-c^2=-a b \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{a^2+b^2-c^2}{2 a b}=-\frac{1}{2} \\ & \Rightarrow \cos \mathrm{C}=\frac{1}{2} \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{C}=\frac{2 \pi}{3} \\ & \Rightarrow \text { circumradius }=\frac{c}{2 \sin C}=\frac{c}{2 \sin \left(\frac{2 \pi}{3}\right)}=\frac{c}{\sqrt{3}} \\ & \end{aligned}$$

content: If the vertices of a triangle are $$(-2,3),(6,-1)$$ and $$(4,3)$$, then the co-ordinates of the circumcentre of the triangle are
explanation:
Here, $$\mathrm{A}(-2,3), \mathrm{B}(6,-1), \mathrm{C}(4,3)$$ are the vertices of $$\triangle A B C$$.

Let $$\mathrm{F}$$ be the circumcentre of $$\triangle \mathrm{ABC}$$.

Let $$F D$$ and $$F E$$ be the perpendicular bisectors of the sides $\mathrm{BC}$ and $$\mathrm{AC}$$ respectively.

$$\therefore \quad \mathrm{D}$$ and $$\mathrm{E}$$ are the midpoints of side $$\mathrm{BC}$$ and $$\mathrm{AC}$$ respectively.

$$\begin{array}{ll} \therefore & D \equiv\left(\frac{6+4}{2}, \frac{-1+3}{2}\right) \\ \therefore & D=(5,1) \\ & \text { and } E \equiv\left(\frac{-2+4}{2}, \frac{3+3}{2}\right) \\ &\therefore \quad \mathrm{E}=(1,3) \end{array}$$

Now, slope of $$B C=\frac{3-(-1)}{4-6}=\frac{4}{-2}=-2$$

$$\therefore \quad$$ Slope of $$\mathrm{FD}=\frac{1}{2}\quad \ldots[\because \mathrm{FD} \perp \mathrm{BC}]$$

Since FD passes through $$(5,1)$$ and has slope $$\frac{1}{2}$$, equation of $$\mathrm{FD}$$ is

$$\begin{aligned} & y-1=\frac{1}{2}(x-5) \\ \therefore \quad & 2(y-1)=x-5 \\ \therefore \quad & 2 y-2=x-5 \\ \therefore \quad & x-2 y-3=0 \quad \text{.... (i)} \end{aligned}$$

Since both the points $$\mathrm{A}$$ and $$\mathrm{C}$$ have same $$y$$ co-ordinates i.e. 3, the given points lie on the line $$y=3$$.

Since the equation $$\mathrm{FE}$$ passes through $$\mathrm{E}(1,3)$$, the equation of $$\mathrm{FE}$$ is $$x=1$$. ..... (ii)

To find co-ordinates of circumcentre, we have to solve equations (i) and (ii).

Substituting the value of $$x$$ in (i), we get

$$\begin{aligned} & 1-2 y-3=0 \\ \therefore \quad & y=-1 \end{aligned}$$

$$\therefore \quad$$ Co-ordinates of circumcentre $$\mathrm{F} \equiv(1,-1)$$.

content: In triangle $$\mathrm{ABC}$$ with usual notations $$\mathrm{b}=\sqrt{3}, \mathrm{c}=1, \mathrm{~m} \angle \mathrm{A}=30^{\circ}$$, then the largest angle of the triangle is
explanation:
By cosine rule, we get

$$\begin{aligned} a^2 & =b^2+c^2-2 b c \cos A \\ & =(\sqrt{3})^2+(1)^2-2(\sqrt{3})(1) \cos \left(30^{\circ}\right) \\ & =3+1-2 \sqrt{3}\left(\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right) \\ & =4-3 \\ & =1 \end{aligned}$$

$$\therefore \quad \mathrm{a}=1$$

$$\therefore \quad$$ Largest angle is angle $$\mathrm{B}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \cos B=\frac{\mathrm{c}^2+\mathrm{a}^2-\mathrm{b}^2}{2 \mathrm{ca}}=\frac{1+1-3}{2 \times 1 \times 1}=\frac{-1}{2} \\ & \therefore \quad B=120^{\circ} \end{aligned}$$

content: If the angles of a triangle are in the ratio $$4: 1: 1$$, then the ratio of the longest side to its perimeter is
explanation:
Let the angles of the triangle be $$4 x, x$$ and $$x$$.

$$\begin{aligned} \therefore \quad & 4 x+x+x=180^{\circ} \Rightarrow 6 x=180^{\circ} \Rightarrow x=30^{\circ} \\ & \frac{\sin 120^{\circ}}{a}=\frac{\sin 30^{\circ}}{b}=\frac{\sin 30^{\circ}}{c} \\ \therefore \quad & a:(a+b+c) \\ & =\left(\sin 120^{\circ}\right):\left(\sin 120^{\circ}+\sin 30^{\circ}+\sin 30^{\circ}\right) \\ & =\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}: \frac{\sqrt{3}+2}{2}=\sqrt{3}:(\sqrt{3}+2) \end{aligned}$$

content: If in $$\triangle \mathrm{ABC}$$, with usual notations, $$a \cdot \cos ^2 \frac{C}{2}+c \cos ^2 \frac{A}{2}=\frac{3 b}{2}$$, then
