explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} & a \cdot \cos ^2 \frac{C}{2}+c \cos ^2 \frac{A}{2}=\frac{3 b}{2} \\ & \Rightarrow a\left(\frac{1+\cos C}{2}\right)+c\left(\frac{1+\cos A}{2}\right)=\frac{3 b}{2} \\ & \Rightarrow a+a \cos C+c+c \cos A=3 b \\ & \Rightarrow a+b+c=3 b \quad \ldots[\because b=c \cos A+a \cos C] \\ & \Rightarrow a+c=2 b \end{aligned}$$

$$\therefore \quad a, b, c$$ are in A.P.

content: In a triangle $$\mathrm{ABC}$$, with usual notations, if $$\mathrm{m} \angle \mathrm{A}=60^{\circ}, \mathrm{b}=8, \mathrm{a}=6$$ and $$\mathrm{B}=\sin ^{-1} x$$, then $$x$$ has the value
explanation:
By sine rule, we get

$$\begin{aligned} & \frac{\sin A}{a}=\frac{\sin B}{b} \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{\sin 60^{\circ}}{6}=\frac{x}{8} \quad \ldots\left[\because B=\sin ^{-1}(x)\right] \\ & \Rightarrow x=\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \times \frac{8}{6} \\ & \Rightarrow x=\frac{2}{\sqrt{3}} \end{aligned}$$

content: In a triangle $$\mathrm{ABC}$$, with usual notations, if $$\mathrm{c}=4$$, then value of $$(a-b)^2 \cos ^2 \frac{C}{2}+(a+b)^2 \sin ^2 \frac{C}{2}$$ is
explanation: $$\begin{aligned} & (a-b)^2 \cos ^2 \frac{C}{2}+(a+b)^2 \sin ^2 \frac{C}{2} \\ & =\left(a^2-2 a b+b^2\right) \cos ^2 \frac{C}{2}+\left(a^2+2 a b+b^2\right) \sin ^2 \frac{C}{2} \\ & =\left(a^2+b^2\right)\left(\cos ^2 \frac{C}{2}+\sin ^2 \frac{C}{2}\right) \\ & \quad-2 a b \cos ^2 \frac{C}{2}+2 a b \sin ^2 \frac{C}{2} \\ & =a^2+b^2-2 a b\left(\cos ^2 \frac{C}{2}-\sin ^2 \frac{C}{2}\right) \\ & =a^2+b^2-2 a b \cdot \cos ^2 \\ & =a^2+b^2-\left(a^2+b^2-c^2\right) \quad \ldots[B y \text { Cosine rule }] \\ & =c^2=4^2=16 \end{aligned}$$

content: If one side of a triangle is double the other and the angles opposite to these sides differ by $$60^{\circ}$$, then the triangle is
explanation:
In $$\triangle \mathrm{ABC}$$, by sine rule,

$$\frac{\sin A}{a}=\frac{\sin B}{b}=\frac{\sin C}{c}$$

According to the given condition,

In $$\triangle \mathrm{ABC}, \mathrm{a}=2 \mathrm{~b}$$ and

$$\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{A}-\mathrm{B}=60^{\circ} \Rightarrow \mathrm{A}=60^{\circ}+\mathrm{B} \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{\sin \left(60^{\circ}+\mathrm{B}\right)}{2 \mathrm{~b}}=\frac{\sin \mathrm{B}}{\mathrm{b}} \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{\sin \mathrm{B}}{\sin \left(\mathrm{B}+60^{\circ}\right)}=\frac{1}{2} \\ & \Rightarrow 2 \sin \mathrm{B}=\sin \mathrm{B} \cos 60^{\circ}+\cos \mathrm{B} \sin 60^{\circ} \\ & \Rightarrow 2 \sin \mathrm{B}=\sin \mathrm{B}\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)+\cos \mathrm{B}\left(\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right) \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{3}{2} \sin \mathrm{B}=\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \cos \mathrm{B} \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow \tan B=\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}} \Rightarrow B=30^{\circ} \\ & \therefore \quad A=30^{\circ}+60^{\circ}=90^{\circ} \end{aligned}$$

$$\therefore \quad \triangle \mathrm{ABC}$$ is right angled.

content: In $$\triangle \mathrm{PQR}, \sin \mathrm{P}, \sin \mathrm{Q}$$ and $$\sin \mathrm{R}$$ are in A.P., then
explanation:
Let $$\mathrm{p}_1, \mathrm{p}_2, \mathrm{p}_3$$ be the altitudes of $$\triangle \mathrm{PQR}$$ Area of $$\triangle \mathrm{PQR}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & =\frac{1}{2} \times \text { base } \times \text { height } \\ & =\frac{1}{2} \times \mathrm{p}_1 \times \mathrm{a} \end{aligned}$$

Area $$=\frac{1}{2} p_1 a$$

$$\therefore \quad \mathrm{p}_1=2 \times \frac{\text { Area }}{\mathrm{a}}$$ ..... (i)

$$\therefore \quad$$ similarly,

$$\mathrm{p}_2=\frac{2 \times \text { Area }}{\mathrm{b}}$$ ..... (ii)

$$\mathrm{p}_3=\frac{2 \times \text { Area }}{\mathrm{c}}$$ ..... (iii)

$$\therefore \quad$$ By sine Rule,

$$\frac{\mathrm{a}}{\sin \mathrm{P}}=\frac{\mathrm{b}}{\sin \mathrm{Q}}=\frac{\mathrm{c}}{\sin \mathrm{R}}$$

Let $$\frac{a}{\sin P}=\frac{b}{\sin Q}=\frac{c}{\sin R}=k$$

$$\therefore \quad \sin \mathrm{P}=\frac{\mathrm{a}}{\mathrm{k}}, \sin \mathrm{Q}=\frac{\mathrm{b}}{\mathrm{k}}, \sin \mathrm{R}=\frac{\mathrm{c}}{\mathrm{k}}$$

$$\sin P, \sin Q$$ and $$\sin R$$ are in A.P.

$$\therefore \quad$$ a, b, c are in A.P.

$$\therefore \quad \frac{1}{a}, \frac{1}{b}, \frac{1}{c}$$ are in H.P. ..... (iv)

From equations (i), (ii), (iii) and (iv), we get $$\mathrm{p}_1, \mathrm{p}_2$$ and $$\mathrm{p}_3$$ are in H.P.

content: Let $$a, b, c$$ be the lengths of sides of triangle $$A B C$$ such that $$\frac{a+b}{7}=\frac{b+c}{8}=\frac{c+a}{9}=k$$. Then $$\frac{(\mathrm{A}(\triangle \mathrm{ABC}))^2}{\mathrm{k}^4}=$$
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} & \text { In } \triangle A B C \\ & \frac{a+b}{7}=\frac{b+c}{8}=\frac{c+a}{9}=k \quad \text{... [Given]}\\ & \therefore \quad a+b=7 k \quad \text{.... (i)}\\ & b+c=8 k \quad \text{.... (ii)}\\ & c+a=9 k \quad \text{.... (iii)} \end{aligned}$$

Adding above equations,

$$\begin{aligned} & 2 \mathrm{a}+2 \mathrm{~b}+2 \mathrm{c}=24 \mathrm{k} \\ & \mathrm{a}+\mathrm{b}+\mathrm{c}=12 \mathrm{k} \quad \text{.... (iv)} \end{aligned}$$

Solving equations (i), (ii), (iii), (iv) We get,

$$\begin{aligned} & \quad \mathrm{c}=5 \mathrm{k}, \mathrm{a}=4 \mathrm{k}, \mathrm{b}=3 \mathrm{k} \\ & \therefore \quad \mathrm{c}^2=\mathrm{a}^2+\mathrm{b}^2 \end{aligned}$$

$$\therefore \quad \triangle \mathrm{ABC}$$ is right angled triangle

$$\therefore \quad \angle \mathrm{C}=90^{\circ}$$

$$\begin{aligned} \begin{aligned} \text { Area of } \triangle \mathrm{ABC} =\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{ab} \sin \mathrm{C} \\ =\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{ab} \sin 90 \\ =\frac{1}{2} \times 4 \mathrm{k} \times 3 \mathrm{k} =6 \mathrm{k}^2 \end{aligned} \\ \therefore \quad \text { Now, } \frac{[\mathrm{A}(\triangle \mathrm{ABC})]^2}{\mathrm{k}^4}=\frac{\left(6 \mathrm{k}^2\right)^2}{\mathrm{k}^4}=\frac{36 \mathrm{k}^4}{\mathrm{k}^4}=36 \end{aligned}$$

content: In $$\triangle \mathrm{ABC}$$, with usual notations, $$\mathrm{m} \angle \mathrm{C}=\frac{\pi}{2}$$, if $$\tan \left(\frac{A}{2}\right)$$ and $$\tan \left(\frac{B}{2}\right)$$ are the roots of the equation $$a_1 x^2+b_1 x+c_1=0\left(a_1 \neq 0\right)$$, then
explanation:
$$\begin{array}{ll} & \text { In } \triangle \mathrm{ABC}, \\ & \angle \mathrm{A}+\angle \mathrm{B}+\angle \mathrm{C}=180^{\circ} \\ \therefore \quad & \angle \mathrm{A}+\frac{\pi}{2}+\angle \mathrm{B}=180^{\circ} \\ \therefore \quad & \angle \mathrm{A}+\angle \mathrm{B}=\frac{\pi}{2} \\ \therefore \quad & \frac{\angle \mathrm{A}}{2}+\frac{\angle \mathrm{B}}{2}=\frac{\pi}{4} \\ & \tan \left(\frac{\mathrm{A}}{2}\right) \text { and } \tan \left(\frac{\mathrm{B}}{2}\right) \text { are roots of equation } \\ & \mathrm{a}_1 x^2+\mathrm{b}_1 x+\mathrm{c}_1=0 \quad \text{... [Given]}\\ \therefore \quad & \operatorname{Sum} \text { of roots }=\frac{-\mathrm{b}_1}{\mathrm{a}_1} \\ & \tan \left(\frac{\mathrm{A}}{2}\right)+\tan \left(\frac{\mathrm{B}}{2}\right)=\frac{-\mathrm{b}_1}{\mathrm{a}_1} \end{array}$$

Also, $$\tan \left(\frac{\mathrm{A}}{2}\right) \cdot \tan \left(\frac{\mathrm{B}}{2}\right)=\frac{\mathrm{c}_1}{\mathrm{a}_1}$$

Using $$\tan \left( {{A \over 2} + {B \over 2}} \right) = {{\tan {A \over 2} + \tan {B \over 2}} \over {1 - \tan {A \over 2}\tan {B \over 2}}}$$, we get

$$\tan \left( {{\pi \over 4}} \right) = {{{{ - {b_1}} \over {{a_1}}}} \over {1 - {{{c_1}} \over {{a_1}}}}}$$

$$1 = {{ - {b_1}} \over {{a_1} - {c_1}}}$$

$${a_1} - {c_1} = - {b_1}$$

$${a_1} + {b_1} = {c_1}$$

content: Two sides of a triangle are $$\sqrt{3}+1$$ and $$\sqrt{3}-1$$ and the included angle is $$60^{\circ}$$, then the difference of the remaining angles is
explanation:
Let $$a=\sqrt{3}+1, b=\sqrt{3}-1, C=60^{\circ}$$

Using cosine Rule,

$$\begin{aligned} & c^2=a^2+b^2-2 a b \cos C \\ & c^2=(\sqrt{3}+1)^2+(\sqrt{3}-1)^2-2(\sqrt{3}+1)(\sqrt{3}-1) \frac{1}{2} \\ & c^2=6 \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} \therefore \quad c^2 & =6 \\ c & =\sqrt{6} \end{aligned}$$

Using sine Rule,

$$\frac{a}{\sin A}=\frac{b}{\sin B}=\frac{c}{\sin C} $$

Consider

$$\begin{array}{ll} & \frac{b}{\sin B}=\frac{c}{\sin C} \\ \therefore & \frac{\sqrt{3}-1}{\sin B}=\frac{\sqrt{6}}{\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}} \\ \therefore & \sin B=\frac{\sqrt{3}-1}{2 \sqrt{2}} \\ \therefore & \angle B=15^{\circ} \\ \therefore & \angle A=105^{\circ} \end{array}$$

.... [Remaining angle of a Triangle]

$$\therefore \quad \text { Difference }=90^{\circ}$$

content: In a triangle ABC with usual notations a = 2, b = 3, then value of $$\frac{\cos 2 \mathrm{~A}}{\mathrm{a}^2}-\frac{\cos 2 \mathrm{~B}}{\mathrm{~b}^2}$$ is
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} & \frac{\cos 2 A}{a^2}-\frac{\cos 2 B}{b^2} \\ & =\frac{1-2 \sin ^2 A}{a^2}-\frac{1-2 \sin ^2 B}{b^2}=\left(\frac{1}{a^2}-\frac{1}{b^2}\right)-2\left(\frac{\sin ^2 A}{a^2}-\frac{\sin ^2 B}{b^2}\right) \end{aligned}$$

From sine rule, we know that

$$\frac{\mathrm{a}}{\sin \mathrm{A}}=\frac{\mathrm{b}}{\sin \mathrm{B}}$$

$$\therefore$$ Given Expression $$=\left(\frac{1}{\mathrm{a}^2}-\frac{1}{\mathrm{~b}^2}\right)-0=\frac{1}{2^2}-\frac{1}{3^2} \quad=\frac{1}{4}-\frac{1}{9}=\frac{5}{36}$$

content: In any $$\triangle A B C$$, with usual notations, $$c(a \cos B-b \cos A)=$$
explanation:
Let's solve the given expression step by step. We are given:

In any $$\triangle ABC$$, with usual notations, find the value of $$c(a \cos B - b \cos A)$$.

We know from the law of cosines that:

$$a^2 = b^2 + c^2 - 2bc \cos A$$

$$b^2 = a^2 + c^2 - 2ac \cos B$$

Rearranging these equations to find $$\cos A$$ and $$\cos B$$, we get:

$$\cos A = \frac{b^2 + c^2 - a^2}{2bc}$$

$$\cos B = \frac{a^2 + c^2 - b^2}{2ac}$$

Using these in the given expression, we have:

$$c(a \cos B - b \cos A) = c \left(a \frac{a^2 + c^2 - b^2}{2ac} - b \frac{b^2 + c^2 - a^2}{2bc}\right)$$

Simplify this further:

$$= c \left(\frac{a(a^2 + c^2 - b^2)}{2ac} - \frac{b(b^2 + c^2 - a^2)}{2bc}\right)$$

Multiplying through by the common denominator (2ac \cdot bc), we get:

$$= \frac{c (a^2 a + ac^2 - ab^2 - b^2 b - bc^2 + ba^2)}{2acbc}$$

Simplifying inside the numerator gives:

$$= \frac{a^3 + ac^2 - ab^2 - b^3 - bc^2 + ab^2}{2acbc}$$

Notice that the terms involving $$ab^2$$ cancel out:

$$= \frac{a^3 + ac^2 - b^3 - bc^2}{2acbc}$$

Grouping the terms with $$c^2$$ together, we get:

$$= a^2 - b^2$$

Thus, the correct option is:

Option A

$$a^2 - b^2$$

content: If in a $$\triangle A B C$$, with usual notations, $$\mathrm{a}^2, \mathrm{~b}^2, \mathrm{c}^2$$ are in A.P. then $$\frac{\sin 3 B}{\sin B}=$$
explanation:
We have $$2 \mathrm{~b}^2=\mathrm{a}^2+\mathrm{c}^2$$

$$\begin{aligned} \frac{\sin 3 B}{\sin B} & =\frac{3 \sin B-4 \sin ^3 B}{\sin B} \\ & =3-4 \sin ^2 B=3-4\left(1-\cos ^2 B\right)=4 \cos ^2 B-1 \\ & =4\left[\frac{c^2+a^2-b^2}{2 a c}\right]^2-1=4\left[\frac{b^2}{2 a c}\right]^2-1 \\ & =\left(\frac{2 b^2}{2 a c}\right)^2-1=\left(\frac{a^2+c^2}{2 a c}\right)^2-1=\left(\frac{a^2+c^2}{2 a c}+1\right)\left(\frac{a^2+c^2}{2 a c}-1\right)=\frac{(a+c)^2(a-c)^2}{(2 a c)^2} \\ & =\left[\frac{(a+c)(a-c)}{2 a c}\right]^2=\left(\frac{a^2-c^2}{2 a c}\right)^2 \end{aligned}$$

content: If $$\mathrm{G}(\overline{\mathrm{g}}), \mathrm{H}(\overline{\mathrm{h}})$$ and $$\mathrm{P}(\overline{\mathrm{p}})$$ are respectively centroid, orthocenter and circumcentre of a triangle and $$\mathrm{x} \overline{\mathrm{p}}+\mathrm{y} \overline{\mathrm{h}}+z \overline{\mathrm{g}}=\overline{0}$$, then $$\mathrm{x}, \mathrm{y}, \mathrm{z}$$ are respectively.
explanation:
We know that centroid divides a line joining orthocenter to circumcentre in the ratio $$2: 1$$.

$$\therefore \overline{\mathrm{g}}=\frac{\overline{\mathrm{h}}+2 \overline{\mathrm{p}}}{1+2} \Rightarrow 2 \overline{\mathrm{p}}+\overline{\mathrm{h}}-3 \overline{\mathrm{g}}=0$$

$$\therefore x=2, y=1, z=-3$$ as per data given.

content: With usual notations in $$\triangle$$ABC, if $$\frac{\sin A}{\sin C}=\frac{\sin (A-B)}{\sin (B-C)}$$, then $$a^2, b^2, c^2$$ are in
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} & \frac{\sin A}{\sin C}=\frac{\sin (A-B)}{\sin (B-C)} \\ & \therefore \sin A(\sin B \cos C-\cos B \sin C)=\sin C(\sin A \cos B-\cos A \sin B) \\ & \therefore \sin A \sin B \cos C+\cos A \sin B \sin C \quad=2 \sin A \cos B \sin C \\ & \therefore \sin B(\sin A \cos C+\cos A \sin C)=2 \sin A \cos B \sin C \\ & \therefore \sin B[\sin (A+C)]=2 \sin A \cos B \sin C \\ & \therefore \sin B \sin B=2 \sin A \cos B \sin C \quad \cdots[\because A+B+C=\pi] \end{aligned}$$

$$\therefore \frac{1}{2 \cos B}=\frac{\sin A}{\sin B} \times \frac{\sin C}{\sin B}$$

By sine rule, we know that $$\frac{a}{\sin A}=\frac{b}{\sin B}=\frac{c}{\sin C}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \therefore \frac{1}{2\left(\frac{c^2+a^2-b^2}{2 a c}\right)}=\frac{a}{b} \times \frac{c}{b} \Rightarrow \frac{a c}{c^2+a^2-b^2}=\frac{a c}{b^2} \\ & \therefore c^2+a^2-b^2=b^2 \Rightarrow 2 b^2=a^2+c^2 \end{aligned}$$

content: The area of the triangle $$\mathrm{ABC}$$ is $$10 \sqrt{3} \mathrm{~cm}^2$$, angle $$\mathrm{B}$$ is $$60^{\circ}$$ and its perimeter is $$20 \mathrm{~cm}$$, then $$\ell(\mathrm{AC})=$$
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} & \text { Area }=\frac{\text { acsin } B}{2} \Rightarrow 10 \sqrt{3}=\frac{\text { acsin } 60}{2} \\ & 10 \sqrt{3}=\frac{a c \sqrt{3}}{4} \Rightarrow \text { ac }=40 \end{aligned}$$

Now

$$\begin{aligned} & \cos B=\frac{a^2+c^2-b^2}{2 a c} \\ & \cos 60 \times 2 a c \quad=a^2+c^2-b^2 \\ \therefore \quad & \frac{1}{2} \times 2 \times 40=(a+c)^2-2 a c-b^2 \\ \therefore \quad 40 & =(20-b)^2-(2 \times 40)-b^2 \quad \text{...[a+b+c=20, given]}\\ & =400+b^2-40 b-80-b^2 \\ \therefore \quad 40 b & =280 \Rightarrow b=7 \end{aligned}$$

content: In a triangle $$\mathrm{ABC}$$, with usual notations $$\mathrm{a}=2, \mathrm{~b}=3, \mathrm{c}=5$$, then $$\frac{\cos \mathrm{A}}{\mathrm{a}}+\frac{\cos \mathrm{B}}{\mathrm{b}}+\frac{\cos \mathrm{C}}{\mathrm{c}}=$$
In $$\Delta ABC$$, with usual notations $$\mathrm{\frac{b\sin B-c\sin C}{\sin(B-C)}}=$$
explanation: $$\begin{aligned} & \frac{a}{\sin A}=\frac{b}{\sin B}=\frac{c}{\sin C}=k \quad \text{... (let)}\\ & \therefore \mathrm{a}=\mathrm{k} \sin \mathrm{A}, \mathrm{b}=\mathrm{k} \sin \mathrm{B}, \mathrm{c}=\mathrm{k} \sin \mathrm{C} \\ & \therefore \frac{\mathrm{b} \sin \mathrm{B}-\mathrm{c} \sin \mathrm{C}}{\sin (\mathrm{B}-\mathrm{C})}=\frac{\mathrm{k} \sin ^2 \mathrm{~B}-\mathrm{k} \sin ^2 \mathrm{C}}{\sin (\mathrm{B}-\mathrm{C})}=\frac{\mathrm{k}\left[\sin ^2 \mathrm{~B}-\sin ^2 \mathrm{C}\right]}{\sin (\mathrm{B}-\mathrm{C})} \\ & =\frac{\mathrm{k} \sin (\mathrm{B}-\mathrm{C}) \sin (\mathrm{B}+\mathrm{C})}{\sin (\mathrm{B}-\mathrm{C})} \\ & =\mathrm{k} \sin (\mathrm{B}+\mathrm{C})=\mathrm{k}[\sin (\pi-\mathrm{A})]=\mathrm{k} \sin \mathrm{A}=\mathrm{a} \\ \end{aligned}$$

content: With usual notations, in any $$\triangle A B C$$, if $$a\cos B=b \cos A$$, then the triangle is
explanation:
We know that $$\frac{a}{\sin A}=\frac{b}{\sin B}$$

$$\therefore a \sin B=b \sin A\quad \text{.... (1)}$$ and we have

$$a \cos B=b \cos A\quad \text{.... (2)}$$

From (1) and (2), we write

$$\frac{a}{b}=\frac{\sin A}{\sin B}=\frac{\cos A}{\cos B} \Rightarrow \tan A=\tan B \Rightarrow A=B$$

Thus $$\Delta$$ is an isosceles triangle.

content: In $$\triangle A B C$$, with usual notations, $$2 a b \sin \frac{1}{2}(A+B-C)=$$
explanation:
We know that in any triangle ABC, the sum of angles is $$180^\circ$$. Therefore, we have:

$$A + B + C = 180^\circ$$

This implies:

$$A + B - C = 180^\circ - 2C$$

Now, let's use the sine rule, which states:

$$\frac{a}{\sin A} = \frac{b}{\sin B} = \frac{c}{\sin C} = k$$

From this, we get:

$$\sin A = \frac{a}{k}, \sin B = \frac{b}{k}, \sin C = \frac{c}{k}$$

Now, we can use the formula for the sine of half an angle:

$$\sin \frac{1}{2} (A + B - C) = \sin \frac{1}{2} (180^\circ - 2C) = \sin (90^\circ - C) = \cos C$$

We can express $$\cos C$$ using the cosine rule:

$$\cos C = \frac{a^2 + b^2 - c^2}{2ab}$$

Now, let's substitute all these values into the given expression:

$$2ab \sin \frac{1}{2} (A + B - C) = 2ab \cos C = 2ab \cdot \frac{a^2 + b^2 - c^2}{2ab} = a^2 + b^2 - c^2$$

Therefore, the correct answer is Option B: $$a^2 + b^2 - c^2$$.

content: If in $$\Delta$$ABC, with usual notations, the angles are in A.P., then $$\mathrm{\frac{a}{c}}$$ sin 2 C + $$\mathrm{\frac{c}{a}}$$ sin 2 A =
explanation:
Angles A, B, C of $$\triangle \mathrm{ABC}$$ are in A.P.

$$\begin{aligned} & \therefore \angle B=60^{\circ} \Rightarrow \angle A+\angle C=120^{\circ} \\ & \text { Also } \frac{a}{\sin A}=\frac{b}{\sin B}=\frac{c}{\sin C}=k \quad \ldots \text { (let) }\\ & \therefore a=k \sin A \quad \& \quad c=k\sin C \\ & \frac{a}{c} \sin 2 C+\frac{c}{a} \sin 2 A \\ & =\frac{k \sin A}{k \sin C}(2 \sin C \cos C)+\frac{k \sin C}{k \sin A}(2 \sin A \cos A) \\ & =2 \sin A \cos C+2 \cos A \sin C \quad=2(\sin A \cos C+\cos A \sin C) \\ & =2 \sin (A+C)=2 \sin \left(120^{\circ}\right)=2\left(\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right)=\sqrt{3} \end{aligned}$$

content: With usual notations, perimeter of a triangle $$A B C$$ is 6 times the arithmetic mean of sine of its angles. If $$\mathrm{a}=1$$, then measure of angle $$\mathrm{A}=$$
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} & \text { Let } \frac{a}{\sin A}=\frac{b}{\sin B}=\frac{c}{\sin C}=k \\ & \therefore \sin A=\frac{a}{k}, \sin B=\frac{b}{k}, \sin C=\frac{c}{k} \end{aligned}$$

With usual notations, from the given data, we write

$$\begin{aligned} & a+b+c=6\left[\frac{\left(\frac{a}{k}+\frac{b}{k}+\frac{c}{k}\right)}{3}\right] \\ & \therefore(a+b+c)=\frac{2(a+b+c)}{k} \Rightarrow k=2 \\ & \therefore \sin A=\frac{a}{k}=\frac{1}{2} \Rightarrow A=\frac{\pi^c}{6} \end{aligned}$$

content: With usual notations if the angles of a triangle are in the ratio 1 : 2 : 3, then their corresponding sides are in the ratio.
explanation:
Let the angles be $$\mathrm{x}, 2 \mathrm{x}, 3 \mathrm{x}$$

$$\therefore \mathrm{x}+2 \mathrm{x}+3 \mathrm{x}=180^{\circ} \Rightarrow 6 \mathrm{x}=180^{\circ} \Rightarrow \mathrm{x}=30^{\circ}$$

Thus angles of the triangle are $$30^{\circ}, 60^{\circ}, 90^{\circ}$$.

Now, $$\frac{a}{\sin A}=\frac{b}{\sin B}=\frac{c}{\sin C}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \therefore \frac{a}{\sin 30^{\circ}}=\frac{b}{\sin 60^{\circ}}=\frac{c}{\sin 90^{\circ}} \Rightarrow \frac{a}{\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)}=\frac{b}{\left(\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right)}=\frac{c}{(1)} \\ & \therefore 2 a=\frac{2 b}{\sqrt{3}}=c \Rightarrow a=\frac{c}{2} \text { and } b=\frac{\sqrt{3} c}{2} \\ & \therefore a: b: c=\frac{c}{2}: \frac{\sqrt{3} c}{2}: c=1: \sqrt{3}: 2 \end{aligned}$$

content: With usual notations, if the angles $A, B, C$ of a $\triangle A B C$ are in $A P$ and $b: c=\sqrt{3}: \sqrt{2}$
explanation:
In $\triangle A B C$,

$\angle A, \angle B, \angle C$ are in A.P

$\therefore \quad 2 \angle B=\angle A+\angle C\quad \text{... (i)}$

and $\angle A+\angle B+\angle C=180^{\circ}\quad\text{.... (ii)}$

From Eqs. (i) and (ii), $B=60^{\circ}$

$$\begin{aligned} & \therefore \quad \frac{b}{\sin B}=\frac{c}{\sin C} \\ & \Rightarrow \quad \frac{\sin B}{\sin C}=\frac{b}{c} \\ & \Rightarrow \quad \frac{\sin 60^{\circ}}{\sin C}=\frac{\sqrt{3}}{\sqrt{2}}[\because \text { given } b: c=\sqrt{3}: \sqrt{2}] \\ & \Rightarrow \quad \sin C=\frac{\sqrt{2} \times \sqrt{3}}{\sqrt{3} \times 2}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \\ & \therefore \quad \sin C=\sin 45^{\circ} \\ & \Rightarrow \quad C=45^{\circ} \\ & \angle A=2 \angle B-\angle C=120^{\circ}-45^{\circ}=75^{\circ} \end{aligned}$$

content: The area of the $\triangle A B C$ is $10 \sqrt{3} \mathrm{~cm}^2$, angle $B$ is $60^{\circ}$ and its perimeter is 20 cm , then $\ell(A C)=$
explanation:
We have,

Area of triangle $A B C=10 \sqrt{3} \mathrm{~cm}^2$

Perimeter of triangle $A B C=20 \mathrm{~cm}$

$$\angle B=60^{\circ}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \text { Area of } \triangle A B C=\frac{1}{2} a c \sin B \\ & 10 \sqrt{3}=\frac{1}{2} a c \times \sin 60^{\circ} \\ & 10 \sqrt{3}=\frac{1}{2} a c \times \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \\ & a c=40 \\ & \cos \frac{B}{2}=\sqrt{\frac{s(s-b)}{a c}} \\ & \therefore \quad \cos 30^{\circ}=\sqrt{\frac{10(10-b)}{40}} \\ & {\left[\because s=\frac{a+b+c}{2}=\frac{20}{2}=10\right]} \\ & \Rightarrow \quad \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}=\frac{\sqrt{(10-b)}}{2} \\ & \Rightarrow \quad 3=(10-b) \\ & \Rightarrow \quad b=10-3=7 \mathrm{~cm} \end{aligned}$$

content: In a $$\triangle A B C$$ if $$2 \cos C=\sin B \cdot \operatorname{cosec} A$$, then
explanation:
We have,

$$\begin{aligned} & 2 \cos C=\sin B \operatorname{cosec} A \\ & \Rightarrow \quad 2 \sin A \cos C=\sin B \\ & \Rightarrow \quad \sin (A+C)+\sin (A-C)=\sin B \\ & \Rightarrow \sin (\pi-B)+\sin (A-C)=\sin B[\because A+B+C=x] \\ & \Rightarrow \quad \sin B+\sin (A-C)=\sin B \\ & \Rightarrow \quad \sin (A-C)=0 \end{aligned}$$

content: In a triangle $$A B C$$ with usual notations, if $$\frac{\cos A}{a}=\frac{\cos B}{b}=\frac{\cos C}{c}$$, then area of triangle $$A B C$$ with $$a=\sqrt{6}$$ is
explanation:
Given, $$\frac{\cos A}{a}=\frac{\cos B}{b}=\frac{\cos C}{c}\quad \text{..... (i)}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow \frac{b^2+c^2-a^2}{2 a b c}=\frac{a^2+c^2-b^2}{2 a b c}=\frac{a^2+b^2-c^2}{2 a b c} \\ & \Rightarrow b^2+c^2-a^2=a^2+c^2-b^2=a^2+b^2-c^2 \\ & \Rightarrow a=b=c \end{aligned}$$

$$\therefore \triangle A B C$$ is an equilateral triangle

$$\begin{aligned} \therefore \text { Area of } \triangle A B C & =\frac{\sqrt{3}}{4} a^2=\frac{\sqrt{3}}{4}(\sqrt{6})^2 \\ & =\frac{\sqrt{3} \times 6}{4}=\frac{3 \sqrt{3}}{2} \text { sq units } \end{aligned}$$

content: In a triangle $$A B C$$, if $$\frac{\sin A-\sin C}{\cos C-\cos A}=\cot B$$, then $$A, B, C$$, are in
explanation:
We have, $$\frac{\sin A-\sin C}{\cos C-\cos A}=\cot B$$

$$\Rightarrow \quad \frac{2 \cos \left(\frac{A+C}{2}\right) \frac{\sin (A-C)}{2}}{2 \sin \left(\frac{C+A}{2}\right) \sin \left(\frac{A-C}{2}\right)}=\cot B$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow \quad \cot \left(\frac{A+C}{2}\right)=\cot B \\ & \Rightarrow \quad A+C=2 B \\ \end{aligned}$$

$$\therefore A, B, C$$ are in Arithmetic progression

content: In $\triangle A B C$, with the usual notations, if $\left(\tan \frac{A}{2}\right)\left(\tan \frac{B}{2}\right)=\frac{3}{4}$ then $a+b=\ldots \ldots$
explanation: $$\begin{aligned} &\text { We have, In } \triangle A B C\\ &\begin{aligned} & \left(\tan \frac{A}{2}\right)\left(\tan \frac{B}{2}\right)=\frac{3}{4} \\ & \Rightarrow \sqrt{\frac{(s-b)(s-c)}{s(s-a)}} \sqrt{\frac{(s-a)(s-c)}{s(s-b)}}=\frac{3}{4} \\ & \Rightarrow \sqrt{\frac{(s-b)(s-c)(s-a)(s-c)}{s(s-a) \cdot s(s-b)}}=\frac{3}{4} \\ & \Rightarrow \quad \frac{(s-c)}{s}=\frac{3}{4} \Rightarrow \frac{\frac{a+b+c}{2}-c}{\frac{a+b+c}{2}}=\frac{3}{4} \\ & \Rightarrow \quad \frac{a+b-c}{a+b+c}=\frac{3}{4} \\ & \Rightarrow \quad 4 a+4 b-4 c=3 a+3 b+3 c \\ & \Rightarrow \quad a+b=7 c \end{aligned} \end{aligned} $$

content: In $\triangle A B C$, with the usual notations, if $\sin B \sin C=\frac{b c}{a^2}$, then the triangle is. ...........
explanation: $$\begin{aligned} & \text { Given, } \sin B \sin C=\frac{b c}{a^2} \\ & \Rightarrow \quad a^2=\frac{b c}{\sin B \sin C} \\ & \Rightarrow \quad a^2=\left(\frac{b}{\sin B}\right)\left(\frac{c}{\sin C}\right) \\ & \Rightarrow \quad a^2=\left(\frac{a}{\sin A}\right)^2 \\ & \Rightarrow \quad \sin ^2 A=1 \\ & \Rightarrow \quad \sin A=1=\sin 90^{\circ} \\ & \Rightarrow \quad A=90^{\circ} \end{aligned}$$

content: If $R$ is the circum radius of $\triangle A B C$, then $A(\triangle A B C)=\ldots \ldots$
explanation:
In any $\triangle A B C$ we know that

Area of $\Delta=\frac{1}{2} b c \sin A$

$\Rightarrow \quad \sin A=\frac{2 \Delta}{b c}\quad\text{.... (i)}$

also, $$R=\frac{a}{2 \sin A}\quad\text{... (ii)}$$

From Eqs. (i) and (ii), we have

$$\begin{aligned} & R=\frac{a}{2\left(\frac{2 \Delta}{b c}\right)} \\ \Rightarrow\quad & R=\frac{a b c}{4 \Delta} \\ \Rightarrow\quad & \Delta=A(\triangle A B C)=\frac{a b c}{4 R} \end{aligned}$$

content: In $\triangle A B C$, if $\tan A+\tan B+\tan C=6$ and $\tan A \cdot \tan B=2$ then $\tan C=$ ...........
explanation:
We have, $\tan A+\tan B+\tan C=6$

$\Rightarrow \tan A \tan B \tan C=6\quad\text{.... (i)}$

and $\tan A \cdot \tan B=2\quad\text{.... (ii)}$

From Eqs. (i) and (ii) we get, $\tan C=3$

content: In $\triangle A B C$; with usual notations, $$\frac{b \sin B-c \sin C}{\sin (B-C)}=\ldots \ldots$$
explanation: $$\begin{aligned} &\begin{aligned} & \text { We have, ss } \frac{b \sin B-c \sin C}{\sin (B-C)} \\ & =\frac{k \sin B \sin B-k \sin C \sin C}{\sin (B-C)} \quad \text { (Using sine rule) }\\ & =\frac{k\left[\sin ^2 B-\sin ^2 C\right.}{\sin (B-C)} \\ & =\frac{k \sin (B+C) \sin (B-C)}{\sin (B-C)} \\ & =k \sin (B+C) \\ & =k \sin \left(180^{\circ}-A\right) \\ & =k \sin A=a \end{aligned}\\ \end{aligned}$$

content: In $\triangle A B C$; with usual notations, if $\cos A=\frac{\sin B}{\sin C}$ then the triangle is ............
explanation: $$\begin{aligned} &\text { We have, }\\ &\begin{array}{ll} & \cos A=\frac{\sin B}{\sin C} \\ \Rightarrow & \frac{b^2+c^2-a^2}{2 b c}=\frac{b}{c}\left(\because \frac{\sin A}{a}=\frac{\sin B}{b}=\frac{\sin C}{c}=k\right) \\ \Rightarrow & b^2+c^2-a^2=2 b^2 \\ \Rightarrow & c^2-a^2=b^2 \\ \Rightarrow & c^2=a^2+b^2 \\ \Rightarrow & \triangle A B C \text { right angled triangle at } \angle C . \end{array} \end{aligned}$$
